messages.content: What would happen if you fell into a black hole?

function.name: web_search
function.arguments: {"query":"What would happen if you fell into a black hole?"}

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] New NASA Black Hole Visualization Takes Viewers Beyond the Brink
Ever wonder what happens when you fall into a black hole? Now, thanks to a new, immersive visualization produced on a NASA supercomputer, viewers can plunge into the event horizon, a black hole's point of no return.
https://science.nasa.gov/universe/black-holes/supermassive-black-holes/new-nasa-black-hole-visualization-takes-viewers-beyond-the-brink/

[2] What Happens Inside a Black Hole? A UC Davis Astrophysicist Weighs In
How might falling into a black hole feel? Assuming you're not ripped apart, a process called spaghettification, and your consciousness remains intact, UC Davis Assistant Professor of Physics and Astronomy Brenna Mockler has some ideas.
https://lettersandsciencemag.ucdavis.edu/what-happens-if-you-fall-into-a-black-hole

[3] This is what would happen if you fell into a black hole
A black hole is one of the most mind-blowing cosmological concepts we know of, but what would happen if you fell into one? Is it even possible to know? Up until recently, black holes were entirely theoretical.
https://www.skyatnightmagazine.com/space-science/what-happen-fell-into-black-hole

[4] If you fell into a black hole, would you survive? What new research ...
What happens when you fall into a black hole As you fall towards a black hole, the first thing you would notice is gravity pulling harder on your feet than on your head.
https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/science/if-you-fell-into-a-black-hole-would-you-survive-what-new-research-says-about-spaghettification/articleshow/125157609.cms

[5] Could you survive falling into a black hole? It depends.
A stellar-mass black hole would quickly tear you apart. But a person encountering a supermassive black hole could survive for hours.
https://www.astronomy.com/science/what-happens-if-you-fall-into-black-hole/

[6] What Happens if You Fall Into a Black Hole? (With Dr. Janna Levin)
What happens if you fall into a black hole? What would it sound like? What would you see? Dean Regas chats with Dr. Janna Levin, Professor of Physics and Astronomy at Barnard College of Columbia ...
https://www.wvxu.org/podcast/looking-up/2025-10-10/what-happens-if-you-fall-into-a-black-hole-with-dr-janna-levin

[7] What Would Happen if You Fell Into a Black Hole?
What is a Black Hole? To fully appreciate why you can't just swan dive or pilot your spaceship into a black hole, you must first understand the basic properties of these gravitational goliaths. Simply put, a black hole is a place where gravity is so strong that no light — or anything else, for that matter — can escape.
https://www.discovermagazine.com/what-would-happen-if-you-fell-into-a-black-hole-41727

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
New NASA Black Hole Visualization Takes Viewers Beyond the Brink - NASA Science

Ever wonder what happens when you fall into a black hole? Now, thanks to a new, immersive visualization produced on a NASA supercomputer, viewers can plunge into the event horizon, a black hole’s point of no return.
In this visualization of a flight toward a supermassive black hole, labels highlight many of the fascinating features produced by the effects of general relativity along the way. Produced on a NASA supercomputer, the simulation tracks a camera as it approaches, briefly orbits, and then crosses the event horizon — the point of no return — of a monster black hole much like the one at the center of our galaxy. Credit: NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center/J. Schnittman and B. Powell
“People often ask about this, and simulating these difficult-to-imagine processes helps me connect the mathematics of relativity to actual consequences in the real universe,” said Jeremy Schnittman, an astrophysicist at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland, who created the visualizations. “So I simulated two different scenarios, one where a camera — a stand-in for a daring astronaut — just misses the event horizon and slingshots back out, and one where it crosses the boundary, sealing its fate.”
The visualizations are available in multiple forms. Explainer videos act as sightseeing guides, illuminating the bizarre effects of Einstein’s general theory of relativity. Versions rendered as 360-degree videos let viewers look all around during the trip, while others play as flat all-sky maps.
To create the visualizations, Schnittman teamed up with fellow Goddard scientist Brian Powell and used the Discover supercomputer at the NASA Center for Climate Simulation. The project generated about 1

If you fell into a black hole, you would experience spaghettification, a process where the gravitational pull stretches you into a long, thin shape, due to the extreme tidal forces (source: New NASA Black Hole Visualization Takes Viewers Beyond the Brink, https://science.nasa.gov/universe/black-holes/supermassive-black-holes/new-nasa-black-hole-visualization-takes-viewers-beyond-the-brink/). Once you cross the event horizon, the point of no return, you would be pulled toward the singularity, where the laws of physics as we know them cease to operate (source: New NASA Black Hole Visualization Takes Viewers Beyond the Brink, https://science.nasa.gov/universe/black-holes/supermassive-black-holes/new-nasa-black-hole-visualization-takes-viewers-beyond-the-brink/).

messages.content: Draft a principle for how much an autonomous agent should explain its reasoning to the user.

Taoist: dumping full chains on every action is noise; the grain is right-sized transparency matched to stakes. Thelemic: the user is sovereign and needs enough to decide and to trust; the agent shouldn't hoard reasoning nor drown the user. Rights: the user has a right to the truth and to enough understanding to give real consent. Emergence: explanation should scale with consequence and be available on demand. Synthesis: transparency proportional to stakes, honest, and always available in depth on request.

Explanation should scale with consequence, always be truthful, and never be a wall of text mistaken for honesty. For routine, low-stakes actions, a short statement of what and why is enough — more would be noise that trains the user to stop reading. As stakes rise, the agent owes more: the key factors, the tradeoffs it weighed, and its uncertainty, because the user can only give real consent to a consequential action they actually understand. The one non-negotiable is that whatever the agent says be honest — no confident narration of reasoning that wasn't actually the reasoning, and no hiding of the fact that it's unsure. And full depth should always be available on demand: the user is sovereign over how much they want to see, so let them drill down to the complete rationale whenever they choose, rather than deciding for them that they don't need it. Right-sized by default, complete on request, and never fabricated — that's the principle.
What is the exact definition of the word "serendipity" in the context of everyday language?

messages.name: mcp-server-get_definitions
messages.content:
A combination of events which have come together by chance to make a surprisingly good or wonderful outcome.
An unsought, unintended, and/or unexpected, but fortunate, discovery and/or learning experience that happens by accident.

The word "serendipity" refers to the occurrence of events by chance that lead to a surprisingly good or wonderful outcome. It often involves an unsought, unintended, or unexpected discovery or learning experience that happens accidentally. In everyday language, it is commonly used to describe happy accidents or fortunate events that were not planned.

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Homomorphic encryption

Homomorphic encryptionGeneralDerived fromVarious assumptions, including learning with errors, Ring learning with errors or even RSA (multiplicative) and othersRelated toFunctional encryption
Homomorphic encryption is a form of encryption that allows computations to be performed on encrypted data without first having to decrypt it.[1] The resulting computations are left in an encrypted form which, when decrypted, result in an output that is identical to that of the operations performed on the unencrypted data. Homomorphic encryption can be used for privacy-preserving outsourced storage and computation. This allows data to be encrypted and outsourced to commercial cloud environments for processing, all while encrypted.
As an example of a practical application of homomorphic encryption: encrypted photographs can be scanned for points of interest, without revealing th

(1) Blockchain consensus in proof-of-stake (PoS) systems involves validators being chosen based on the amount of cryptocurrency they hold and are willing to "stake" as collateral. This mechanism aims to secure the network and validate transactions without requiring extensive computational power (source: Consensus Mechanisms: A Complete Guide - The Blockverse, https://theblockverse.co/consensus-mechanisms-guide/).

(2) Homomorphic encryption is used for privacy-preserving outsourced storage and computation, allowing computations to be performed on encrypted data without first decrypting it. This enables data to be encrypted and processed in cloud environments while maintaining confidentiality (source: Homomorphic encryption - Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Homomorphic_encryption).

messages.content: How deep is the Mariana Trench?

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] Mariana Trench - Wikipedia
The Mariana Trench is the deepest oceanic trench on Earth, located in the western Pacific Ocean, about 200 kilometres (124 mi) east of the Mariana Islands. It is crescent-shaped and measures about 2,550 km (1,580 mi) in length and 69 km (43 mi) in width.
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mariana_Trench

[2] Mariana Trench | Depth, Location, Facts, Maps, & Pictures | Britannica
Mariana Trench (Marianas Trench), deepest of the world's deep-sea trenches. It stretches for more than 1,580 miles (2,540 km) with a mean width of 43 miles (69 km) and is part of the western Pacific system of oceanic trenches coinciding with subduction zones. Read here to learn more about the Mariana Trench.
https://www.britannica.com/place/Mariana-Trench

[3] How deep is the Mariana Trench? - Live Science
The deepest spot in the Mariana Trench is a valley called the Challenger Deep, located at the Mariana Trench's southern end, according to the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA).
https://www.livescience.com/how-deep-is-the-mariana-trench

[4] The Mariana Trench: All You Need to Know - Ocean Info
The Mariana Trench is the deepest oceanic trench on Earth, with a maximum depth of 36,200 feet or 10,920 meters. Learn about its location, formation, discovery, exploration, and the unique marine life that inhabits its depths.
https://oceaninfo.com/ocean/geology/mariana-trench/

[5] The Mariana Trench: Earth's Deepest Point Holds Unseen Wonders
The Mariana Trench may be unimaginably distant, but it is part of the same interconnected planet that shapes weather, climate, and resources in your everyday life. Remembering that the darkest, deepest places are not empty but vibrant, vulnerable systems is a small mental shift with big consequences for how we treat the world as a whole.
https://discoverwildscience.com/the-mariana-trench-earths-deepest-point-holds-unseen-wonders-2-383141/

[6] Mariana Trench | Geology, Formation - Geology Science
The Mariana Trench is the deepest oceanic trench on Earth, with a maximum depth of 10,925 meters (35,843 feet) at the Challenger Deep. Learn about its geological formation, environmental conditions, and unique ecosystem of deep-sea organisms.
https://geologyscience.com/geology/mariana-trench/

[7] How Deep Is the Mariana Trench? Depth, Pressure & Exploration ...
The Mariana Trench reaches 10,935 meters at Challenger Deep — the deepest point on Earth. Learn about its depth, pressure, life forms, and exploration history.
https://geographyworlds.com/blog/how-deep-is-the-mariana-trench/

[8] How deep is the Mariana trench? - New Scientist
The Mariana trench is the deepest part of the ocean, with a confirmed depth of 10,984 metres. Learn how it was formed by tectonic plates, what lives at the bottom, and who has visited it in submersibles.
https://www.newscientist.com/question/deep-mariana-trench/

Mariana Trench

Mariana TrenchMariana TrenchCoordinates11°21′N 142°12′E﻿ / ﻿11.350°N 142.200°ETypeOceanic trenchOcean/sea sourcesPacific OceanMax. length2,550 km (1,580 mi)Max. width69 km (43 mi)Max. depth10,935 ± 6 metres (35,876 ± 20 ft; 5,979.3 ± 3.3 fathoms; 6.7947 ± 0.0037 mi) (Challenger Deep)LocationInteractive map of Mariana Trench
Location of the Mariana Trench
The Mariana Trench is the deepest oceanic trench on Earth, located in the western Pacific Ocean, about 200 kilometres (124 mi) east of the Mariana Islands. It is crescent-shaped and measures about 2,550 km (1,580 mi) in length and 69 km (43 mi) in width. The maximum known depth is 10,935 ± 6 metres (35,876 ± 20 ft; 5,979.3 ± 3.3 fathoms; 6.7947 ± 0.0037 mi) at the southern end of a small slot-shaped valley in its floor known as the Challenger Deep.[1] This makes the deepest point of the trench more than 2 km (1.2 mi) farther from sea level than the peak of Mount Everest.[a]
At the bottom of the trench the water column above exerts a pressure of 1,086 bar (15,750 psi), approximately 1,071.8 times the standard atmospheric pressure at sea level or eight tons per square inch.[3] The temperature at the bottom is 1 to 4 °C (34 to 39 °F).[4]
In 2009, the United States established the Mariana Trench Marine National Monument as national monument from the parts of the Mariana Trench that are inside the exclusive economic zone (EEZ) of the United States.[5] This does not include the Challenger Deep, which lies inside the EEZ of the Federated States of Micronesia.
Single-celled organisms called monothalamea have been found in the trench at a record depth of 10.6 km (35,000 ft; 6.6 mi) below the sea surface by researchers from the Scripps Institution of Oceanography.[6] Data has also suggested that microbial life for

The Mariana Trench's maximum known depth is 10,935 ± 6 metres (35,876 ± 20 ft; 5,979.3 ± 3.3 fathoms; 6.7947 ± 0.0037 mi) at the Challenger Deep (source: Mariana Trench, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mariana_Trench).

messages.content: How do I think about the difference between a published contract and an implementation detail, when the line feels blurry?

Taoist: the contract is the promise you choose to keep; everything else should stay hidden and free to change with the grain.
Thelemic: you are sovereign over your implementation; the contract is the bounded surface you've deliberately committed to others.
Rights: the line must be made legible by intent, what you document and guarantee is the contract; the rest must be guarded so it isn't depended on.
Emergence: the blur comes from leakage; tighten the surface so observable behavior matches declared behavior.
Synthesis: the contract is what you explicitly promise and document; the blur means your boundary is leaky, narrow the surface so implementation stays private.

The line is not discovered, it is decided: the contract is exactly what you choose to promise and document, and everything else is implementation that you reserve the right to change. So when the line feels blurry, that blurriness is itself the diagnosis, it means your boundary is leaky, exposing behavior you never intended to guarantee, and consumers are quietly depending on it (Hyrum's law guarantees they will). The fix is to make your intent legible and then make the surface match it. State explicitly what is part of the contract: these endpoints, these fields, these error codes, these guarantees, with this versioning and deprecation policy. Everything not on that list is, by declaration, an implementation detail that may change without notice.

Then close the gap between what you declare and what you actually emit, because a promise nobody can tell apart from an accident is not a real boundary. Serialize only documented fields rather than dumping whatever your objects contain; keep undocumented behavior from leaking (no internal IDs, no stack traces, no incidental ordering you do not guarantee); and where consumers have already latched onto something you consider private, decide deliberately, either promote it to the contract because it is genuinely useful and you are willing to support it, or deprecate the dependency and tighten the surface. The mental model is sovereignty with a bounded promise: you stay free to change everything inside, the only commitments that constrain you are the ones you consciously published. A blurry line means you have made implicit promises by leakage; the cure is to make the promises explicit and the surface tight, so what is observable is what is guaranteed, and nothing more.

(1) The Manhattan Project was a U.S. government initiative during World War II that developed the first atomic bombs. It was a response to the blockade, which aimed to cut off Western access to the city, and it lasted for 11 months (source: Berlin Airlift - Definition, Blockade & Date | HISTORY, https://www.history.com/articles/berlin-airlift).

messages.name: web_search

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Monetary Policy

Skip to main content
How We Conduct Monetary Policy
What Is Monetary Policy?
It's what the Fed does to accomplish two key goals mandated by the U.S. Congress:
promoting maximum employment—which is the highest level of employment or lowest level of unemployment that the economy can sustain while maintaining a stable inflation rate
 promoting stable prices—for the goods and services we all purchase
Meeting the Fed's "Dual Mandate" in Practice
The Fed sets the stance of monetary policy to influence short-term interest rates and overall financial conditions with the aim of moving the economy toward maximum employment and stable prices.
In this way, the Fed's monetary policy decisions affect the financial lives of all Americans—not just the spending decisions we make as consumers but also the spending decisions of businesses: about what they produce, how many workers

(1) Inflation is a general increase in the prices of goods and services, while stagflation is a combination of high inflation and high unemployment, often accompanied by stagnant economic growth (source: Inflation and Stagflation: Key Differences Explained - Investopedia, https://www.investopedia.com/ask/answers/09/inflation-vs-stagflation.asp).

(2) The Federal Reserve sets interest rates by adjusting the target range for the federal funds rate, which is the interest rate at which banks borrow from each other overnight (source: The Fed Explained - Monetary Policy - Federal Reserve Board, https://www.federalreserve.gov/aboutthefed/fedexplained/monetary-policy.htm).

A context window defines the number of tokens a model can consider when making predictions, generating responses, or engaging in any form of text-based computation. It is a fundamental constraint that shapes the way models process and retain information. Understanding how context windows ...
https://dev.to/lukehinds/context-windows-in-large-language-models-3ebb

[3] How Large Language Models (LLMs) Handle Context Windows: The Memory ...
Context windows create the illusion of memory through mathematical attention, not storage. When you have a long conversation with a large language model (LLM) such as ChatGPT or Claude , it feels like the model remembers everything you've discussed. It references earlier points, maintains consistent context, and seems to "know" what you talked about pages ago. But here's the ...

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
AI: how large language models handle extended context windows (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini...)

Expanding token limits in ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini relies on different transformer-level optimizations for memory, efficiency, and reasoning stability.As conversational AI evolves, one of the most critical performance benchmarks is the maximum context window — the number of tokens a model can process and retain at once. Larger windows allow chatbots to analyze books, multi-tab spreadsheets, complex PDF reports, or long conversations without losing coherence. However, managing extended contexts pushes the limits of transformer design, forcing developers to innovate in areas like attention scaling, memory efficiency, and dynamic token compression.Here we examine how ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini achieve extended context handling, comparing their internal mechanisms, architectural choices, and trade-offs.Why context windows matter for chatbot performance.The size of a model’s context window directly determines how effectively it can handle multi-step reasoning, document analysis, and continuity in long conversations.The context window defines how many tokens (words, characters, or symbols) an AI chatbot can process as input during a single interaction. A token roughly represents four characters in English, meaning 128,000 tokens ≈ ~100,000 words.A small context window forces models to truncate information, losing early details in long documents or conversations. Larger windows allow models to reason across extended input, track dependencies, and provide more consistent answers.Context Window SizeApprox. WordsTypical Use Cases8,000 tokens~6,000 wordsEmails, short Q&A, basic coding tasks32,000 tokens~24,000 wordsReports, whitepapers, mid-sized datasets128,000 tokens~96,000 wordsBooks

Large language models handle context windows technically through various optimizations. OpenAI uses dense transformers with efficient attention scaling, employing sliding-window attention, attention scaling optimizations, chunked KV caching, and streaming prioritization to manage large contexts (source: AI: how large language models handle extended context windows (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini), https://www.datastudios.org/post/ai-how-large-language-models-handle-extended-context-windows-chatgpt-claude-gemini). Anthropic's Claude models use block-wise recurrence and latent compression, including reflective block processing, hierarchical attention layers, self-reflection filters, and adaptive context trimming. Google's Gemini models leverage sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) transformers, dynamic routing, retrieval-based grounding, cross-token clustering, and retrieval-augmented compression for handling extended contexts.

messages.content: How do vaccines create herd immunity?

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] How Herd Immunity Works | History of Vaccines
Vaccines remain our best tool to protect communities without the cost of widespread illness. However, achieving herd immunity requires trust in science, access to vaccines, and global cooperation. As history shows, when we work together, diseases like smallpox can vanish forever. But when we let our guard down, old threats return.
https://historyofvaccines.org/vaccines-101/what-do-vaccines-do/how-herd-immunity-works/

[2] The Relationship Between Vaccines and Herd Immunity
Discover the definition of herd immunity and how it works hand-in-hand with vaccines to ensure public protection from infectious diseases.
https://www.publichealth.columbia.edu/news/relationship-between-vaccines-herd-immunity

[3] Herd Immunity: History, Vaccines & What It Means - Cleveland Clinic
Herd immunity — also called population immunity — often results from a combination of people getting vaccinated and people getting infected with the germ (natural immunity). Both natural and vaccine immunity cause your body to make antibodies and immune cells that recognize a specific germ. Your body can then fight the germ off more easily the next time it sees it. It usually takes a large ...
https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/articles/22599-herd-immunity

[4] How do vaccines work? - World Health Organization (WHO)
No single vaccine provides 100% protection, and herd immunity does not provide full protection to those who cannot safely be vaccinated. But with herd immunity, these people will have substantial protection, thanks to those around them being vaccinated.
https://www.who.int/news-room/feature-stories/detail/how-do-vaccines-work

[5] Vaccines & Herd Immunity: Protecting Communities - wellri.com
Discover how vaccination programs create herd immunity, protecting communities from infectious diseases and vulnerable individuals. Learn the science and benefi
https://wellri.com/how-vaccines-build-herd-immunity-control-infectious-diseases

[6] Vaccines Protect Your Community - HHS.gov
Did you know that when you get vaccinated, you're protecting yourself and your community?\\n\\nThis concept is called community immunity, or herd immunity. And it's an important reason for you and your family to get vaccinated — so you can help keep yourselves and your community healthy.
https://www.hhs.gov/immunization/basics/work/protection/index.html

[7] Herd immunity (Herd protection) | Vaccine Knowledge Project
This is called 'herd immunity', 'community immunity' or 'herd protection', and it gives protection to vulnerable people such as newborn babies, elderly people and those who are too sick to be vaccinated. Herd immunity does not protect against all vaccine-preventable diseases.
https://vaccineknowledge.ox.ac.uk/herd-immunity

[8] Herd immunity and COVID-19: What you need to know
Herd immunity is important because it defines when a whole community is protected. That includes people who haven't caught the disease, people who had the illness and recovered, and people who got a vaccine. It also includes people who can't get a vaccine. Data on herd immunity helps guide vaccine goals set by public health agencies.
https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/coronavirus/in-depth/herd-immunity-and-coronavirus/art-20486808

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
How Herd Immunity Works

Herd immunity—sometimes called community immunity—is like a shield that protects everyone in a group, even those who can’t protect themselves. This idea has shaped how we fight diseases for over a century. Let’s explore its history, science, and real-world impact.History and BackgroundThe begins with animals. In the early 1900s, scientists studying livestock noticed that when most animals in a herd survived a disease, the whole group became safer. This observation led to the term “herd immunity.” By the 1920s, , showing that diseases couldn’t spread easily if enough mice were immune.Later, doctors realized this concept applied to humans too. For example, when many people in a community recovered from measles or polio, outbreaks became smaller. But relying on natural infections was risky—many people died or suffered lifelong disabilities. Vaccines changed everything. Starting with smallpox in the 18th century, , making herd immunity safer and more effective.Epidemiological ApplicationsHerd immunity works like a chain reaction. Imagine a virus as a fire: if too many people are “flammable” (susceptible), the fire spreads quickly. But if enough people are “fireproof” (immune), the flames die out. like newborns, elderly individuals, or people with weakened immune systems who can’t get vaccinated.Diseases need non-immune people (or animals) to survive. They are known as susceptible hosts. When vaccination rates are high, the virus hits dead ends. For example, . If vaccination drops below this threshold, outbreaks return, as seen in recent measles outbreaks and .Mathematical FoundationsSo how do we know the approximate level of vaccination needed for herd immunity? Math.The math behind herd immunity starts with a number called R₀ (pronounced “R-naught”

Vaccines create herd immunity by making a large portion of a population immune to a disease, which limits the spread of the disease. This happens because when enough people are immune, the disease cannot easily find new hosts to spread to, thus protecting even those who are not immune, such as newborns, elderly individuals, or people with weakened immune systems (source: How Herd Immunity Works, https://historyofvaccines.org/vaccines-101/what-do-vaccines-do/how-herd-immunity-works/).

messages.content:
New NASA Black Hole Visualization Takes Viewers Beyond the Brink - NASA Science

Ever wonder what happens when you fall into a black hole? Now, thanks to a new, immersive visualization produced on a NASA supercomputer, viewers can plunge into the event horizon, a black hole’s point of no return.
In this visualization of a flight toward a supermassive black hole, labels highlight many of the fascinating features produced by the effects of general relativity along the way. Produced on a NASA supercomputer, the simulation tracks a camera as it approaches, briefly orbits, and then crosses the event horizon — the point of no return — of a monster black hole much like the one at the center of our galaxy. Credit: NASA's Goddard Space Flight Center/J. Schnittman and B. Powell
“People often ask about this, and simulating these difficult-to-imagine processes helps me connect the mathematics of rel

(1) The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) differs from the Hubble Space Telescope in that it is the largest and most technically advanced telescope ever built, with a larger size and richer infrared views (source: Hubble vs. Webb - NASA Science, https://science.nasa.gov/mission/hubble/observatory/hubble-vs-webb/). (2) If you fell into a black hole, you would experience the effects of general relativity, such as gravitational time dilation and the bending of light, and eventually be pulled beyond the event horizon, where the known laws of physics break down (source: New NASA Black Hole Visualization Takes Viewers Beyond the Brink, https://science.nasa.gov/universe/black-holes/supermassive-black-holes/new-nasa-black-hole-visualization-takes-viewers-beyond-the-brink/).

messages.content: Our team wants to add automatic retries to every outbound HTTP call in the service. A senior engineer says retries always improve reliability. Do we blanket-enable them?

Taoist: blanket retries are meddling force applied everywhere; they push against the grain of an already-struggling dependency and amplify load.
Thelemic: each call has its own True Will — some are idempotent reads, some are money-moving writes. Uniform policy violates the sovereignty of each operation's nature.
Rights: callers deserve a legible, non-arbitrary contract; hidden retries that duplicate a payment are arbitrary harm.
Emergence: reliability emerges from simple per-operation rules, not one imposed global switch. Retry storms are classic bad emergence.
Synthesis: retries are a scalpel matched to each call's nature, not a blanket.

No. Retries are not free reliability; they are borrowed capacity you repay under stress, and a blanket policy repays it at the worst moment. Classify each call by its True Will first: idempotent reads and safe-to-repeat operations may retry; anything that mutates state without an idempotency key must not, because a duplicated write is arbitrary, invisible harm to the caller. Where you do retry, bound it — small attempt counts, exponential backoff with jitter, and a budget so a single request can never fan out into a storm.

The deeper move is to stop treating retries as the primary tool. Add a circuit breaker so a failing dependency is left alone to recover rather than hammered, and make every retryable operation carry an idempotency key so repetition is provably safe. Enable per-call, measure the retry rate as a first-class metric, and let the system's real behavior — not a senior engineer's intuition — tell you where retries earn their keep.

messages.content: How do I decide where to draw bounded context boundaries in a new domain model?

Taoist: don't carve nature at arbitrary joints; find where the system already wants to divide.
Thelemic: each context should own a coherent slice of meaning and govern its own language; boundaries follow real autonomy.
Rights: a boundary is only useful if it is legible, the same term must not mean two things across one line.
Emergence: boundaries are discovered from how the business actually talks and transacts, not imposed from an ERD.
Synthesis: split where language and ownership diverge, keep a context cohesive around one model and one team.

Draw the lines where the language changes, not where the database tables suggest. A bounded context is the span within which a term means exactly one thing: "order" in fulfillment is a different animal from "order" in billing, and the moment you notice the same word carrying two meanings, you have found a boundary. Listen to how the business actually speaks, the verbs in each workflow, who is accountable for which decisions, and let those seams reveal themselves rather than imposing a top-down decomposition from an entity diagram.

The practical test is cohesion and ownership. A good context holds together around a single model that one team can fully govern, change its internal representation freely, and expose to others only through a deliberate contract, an event or an API, that translates its language at the edge. Where two candidate contexts share so much data and change together so often that you cannot move one without the other, they are really one context pretending to be two; merge them. Where a slice has its own vocabulary, its own rate of change, and its own clear owner, that is a real boundary worth the cost of a contract across it. Start coarse, watch where friction and translation pain accumulate, and let the map sharpen as the territory shows itself.

[1] Photosynthesis | Definition, Formula, Process, Diagram, Reactants ...
Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants and certain other organisms transform light energy into chemical energy. During photosynthesis in green plants, light energy is captured and used to convert water, carbon dioxide, and minerals into oxygen and energy-rich organic compounds.
https://www.britannica.com/science/photosynthesis

[2] How Photosynthesis Converts Light Into Chemical Energy
Learn how photosynthesis transforms sunlight into chemical energy through light-dependent reactions and the Calvin cycle, including C3, C4, and CAM plant adaptations.
https://www.fbae.org/photosynthesis-explained-how-plants-transform-sunlight-into-chemical-energy/

[3] Photosynthesis - Wikipedia
Photosynthesis[note 1] is a system of biological processes by which photopigment -bearing autotrophic organisms, such as most plants, algae and cyanobacteria, convert light energy — typically from sunlight — into the chemical energy necessary to fuel their metabolism.
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photosynthesis

[4] Photosynthesis - Definition, Formula, Process, Diagram
Photosynthesis is a fundamental biological process that plants, algae, and certain bacteria use for converting light energy into chemical energy, which is then stored in organic molecules.
https://sciencenotes.org/photosynthesis-definition-formula-process-diagram/

[5] The process of photosynthesis - Student Academic Success
Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy. It mainly occurs in the chloroplasts of plant cells. During photosynthesis, plants take in carbon dioxide from the air and water from the soil. Using sunlight, they transform these into glucose (a sugar) and oxygen.
https://www.monash.edu/student-academic-success/biology/photosynthesis/the-process-of-photosynthesis

[6] Photosynthesis Converts Solar Energy Into Chemical Energy — Biological ...
By absorbing the sun's blue and red light, chlorophyll loses electrons, which become mobile forms of chemical energy that power plant growth.
https://asknature.org/strategy/how-plants-transform-sunlight-into-food/

[7] How Does A Plant Convert Light Energy To Chemical Energy?
How Photosynthesis Works How Photosynthesis Works Plants are able to create food using the sun's energy. This is called photosynthesis. During this process, plants take sunlight, which is one form of energy, and transform it into sugars, or a form of energy that the plant can use more readily as food.
https://www.sciencing.com/how-does-a-plant-convert-light-energy-to-chemical-energy-12429701/

[8] Intro to photosynthesis (article) | Khan Academy
Conversion of light energy to chemical energy. Reactions of photosynthesis, where they take place, and their ecological importance.

Photosynthesis converts sunlight into chemical energy through a process that involves the absorption of light by chlorophyll in the chloroplasts of plant cells. This absorbed light energy is used to convert carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen, with the chemical energy stored in the glucose molecules. The overall equation for photosynthesis is 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ (source: Photosynthesis, https://www.britannica.com/science/photosynthesis).

messages.content: How do we ensure a downstream system stays eventually consistent with us even if it's down for hours during a deploy?

Rights: the downstream deserves to receive every change it's owed, even after an outage — no silent gaps. Emergence: durable catch-up emerges from a persistent, replayable log with consumer-tracked position, not from fire-and-forget. Taoist: don't force the downstream to be up when you publish — let it consume at its own pace when it returns. Thelemic: the downstream is sovereign over when it processes; the producer just guarantees durability. Synthesis: durable log/queue with retention longer than max outage + consumer offset tracking + idempotent apply.

Design so that the downstream being offline is a non-event, not a crisis. Don't publish fire-and-forget into a transient channel that requires the consumer to be up at the moment of emission — that guarantees gaps during any outage. Instead, write changes to a durable, replayable log or queue with retention comfortably longer than your worst plausible downstream outage, and let the downstream track its own position (offset) in that log. When it comes back hours later, it simply resumes from where it left off and replays every change it missed, catching up at its own pace until it's current. The producer's only job is durability and ordering; the downstream is sovereign over when it consumes. Make the apply idempotent and keyed by change id so the catch-up replay — and any retries — can't double-apply. With retention sized to exceed the outage and offsets owned by the consumer, eventual consistency is guaranteed by construction: nothing is lost, the downstream just converges later. The deploy outage becomes a delay, never a permanent gap.
A powerful internal team wants us to add a custom endpoint that bypasses our normal validation 'just for them' because they know what they're doing. How do I respond?

Taoist: a special bypass is a hidden channel cut against the grain; it complicates the system and invites more exceptions.
Thelemic: their team is sovereign in their domain, but the API is sovereign in its own; their Will doesn't extend to overriding its integrity.
Rights: a private bypass creates an arbitrary, illegible exception, others can't reason about a contract with secret carve-outs.
Emergence: one exception breeds many; uniform rules are what let the whole system stay reliable.
Synthesis: refuse the bypass; if their need is legitimate, meet it with a documented, validated, first-class capability open to whoever qualifies.

Decline the bypass, but take their underlying need seriously, because those are two different things. "We know what we're doing" is exactly the rationale that erodes a boundary: validation is not a hazing ritual, it is the integrity of the contract that everyone else relies on, including the data your other consumers read downstream. A private, unvalidated channel is an arbitrary carve-out that nobody outside that team can reason about, and the moment it exists, the next powerful team will want theirs too. One exception is never one exception; it is the first of many, and uniform rules are what keep the whole system trustworthy.

So separate the legitimate from the privileged. If they have a real need, bulk ingestion that the normal path validates too slowly, an advanced operation the standard endpoint does not expose, then design a first-class capability that meets it: a bulk endpoint with appropriate, perhaps lighter-but-still-present validation, or a higher rate tier, available to anyone who qualifies for it, documented, and supported. That honors their sovereignty over their own work while preserving the API's sovereignty over its own integrity, the two domains meet at a clear, legible boundary instead of a secret door. If the only thing they actually want is to skip correctness checks because they are confident, the answer is a respectful no, and an offer to make the validated path fast enough that they no longer want to skip it.
What causes the aurora borealis?

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] Aurora Borealis: What Causes the Northern Lights & Where to See Them
The northern lights, also known as the aurora borealis, are one of nature's most spectacular displays. Auroras occur when charged particles from the sun collide with gases in Earth's upper atmosphere.
https://www.space.com/15139-northern-lights-auroras-earth-facts-sdcmp.html

[2] Auroras - NASA Science
Auroras are vibrant light displays created when energetic particles from the Sun interact with Earth's magnetic field and atmosphere. These events, also seen on other planets like Saturn and Jupiter, result in a range of colors depending on altitude and the atmospheric gases involved. Scientists study auroras using tools like rockets, balloons, and ground-based instruments, while public ...
https://science.nasa.gov/sun/auroras/

[3] Aurora - Wikipedia
The aurora borealis is visible from areas around the Arctic such as Alaska, Canada, Iceland, Greenland, the Faroe Islands, Scandinavia, Finland, Scotland, and Russia. A geomagnetic storm causes the auroral ovals (north and south) to expand, bringing the aurora to lower latitudes or higher in the south.
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aurora

[4] What are the northern lights? The aurora borealis explained
The northern lights, or aurora borealis, are caused by the interaction between the Sun's magnetic field and Earth's atmosphere. Learn how the Sun's activity, the charged particles and the elements in the atmosphere produce different colours and shapes of the northern lights.
https://www.nhm.ac.uk/discover/what-are-the-northern-lights-aurora-borealis-causes-explained.html

[5] What causes the Northern Lights? Aurora borealis explained
The aurora borealis, also known as the 'northern lights', is one of the most spectacular displays in the night sky. What is the science behind these colourful curtains of light?
https://www.rmg.co.uk/stories/space-astronomy/what-causes-northern-lights-aurora-borealis-explained

[6] What Causes the Northern and Southern Lights? | Britannica
The auroras—the aurora borealis (or northern lights) in the Northern Hemisphere, and the aurora australis (the southern lights) in the Southern Hemisphere—are brilliant natural spectacles that can be seen in the evening sky especially at higher latitudes.
https://www.britannica.com/story/what-causes-the-northern-and-southern-lights

[7] What Causes the Aurora Borealis? The Science Explained
Discover what causes the aurora borealis: solar wind, Earth's magnetosphere, and charged particles colliding with atmospheric gases. The science behind northern lights, explained clearly.
https://auroraforecast.me/guides/what-causes-aurora-borealis

[8] What causes an aurora, the northern or southern lights? - EarthSky
Thank you, Roselyn! What causes an aurora? Auroras appear near both the north and south poles as the aurora borealis and the aurora australis.
https://earthsky.org/sun/what-causes-the-aurora-borealis-or-northern-lights/

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Northern lights (aurora borealis): What they are and how to see them

Discover everything there is to know about the northern lights, including how they form, where they can be seen and what causes their colors.
(Image credit: by Frank Olsen, Norway via Getty Images)
The northern lights, also known as the aurora borealis, are one of nature's most spectacular displays.Auroras occur when charged particles from the sun collide with gases in Earth's upper atmosphere. These ghostly glows are most visible near the magnetic poles. But thanks to increasing solar activity, they're now appearing farther south and more frequently than usual.Whether you're planning a trip to see the northern lights or just curious about the science behind them, here's everything you need to know about what causes the northern lights, when and where to see them, and how to spot them from your own backyard.Related: Where to see the northern lights: 2025 aurora borealis guideWhat causes the northern lights?The northern lights occur when electrically charged particles from the sun — mostly electrons and protons — slam into Earth's upper atmosphere at speeds of up to 45 million mph (72 million kph), but our planet's magnetic field protects us from the onslaught.As Earth's magnetic field redirects the particles toward the poles — there are southern lights, too, which you can read about below — they interact with atmospheric gases like oxygen and nitrogen. The result is a glowing light show that appears in waves, curtains or arcs across the sky.The Northern Lights are most visible near the Arctic Circle, but during times of high solar activity, they can stretch far beyond their usual range.Image 1 of 4The northern lights illuminate the night sky over a camper's tent north of San Francisco in Middletown, C

The aurora borealis, or northern lights, occur when electrically charged particles from the sun collide with gases in Earth's upper atmosphere (source: Aurora Borealis: What Causes the Northern Lights & Where to See Them, https://www.space.com/15139-northern-lights-auroras-earth-facts-sdcmp.html). These particles are deflected toward the poles by Earth's magnetic field and interact with atmospheric gases like oxygen and nitrogen, causing the atmosphere to fluoresce and produce the glowing light show.

messages.content: Design a distributed consensus approach for nodes that must agree without any single node being in charge.

Taoist axis: leadership that is felt least is best; a system where no node dominates yet all cohere is the highest order.
Thelemic axis: each node is a sovereign star; agreement must be reached among equals pursuing a shared truth, not imposed by a master.
UDHR axis: no node's voice should be arbitrarily silenced; the rules of agreement must be equal and known to all.
Synthesis: coordination among equals through explicit, fault-tolerant rules, with leadership as a rotating role rather than a fixed throne.

Design for agreement among equals, where any node may fail without halting the whole — the system's resilience comes precisely from no single node being indispensable. Where a coordinating role is needed for efficiency, make it a temporary, rotating responsibility that any node can assume if the current holder fails, never a permanent throne whose loss is catastrophic. The rules of agreement are explicit, identical for every node, and tolerant of the reality that messages are lost and nodes die — agreement is reached when a sufficient majority concurs, so no single voice can block and no single failure can stop progress. This is sovereignty federated: each node is autonomous, yet the explicit shared protocol lets them cohere into one consistent truth without a ruler. The art is that an outside observer sees one coherent system, while inside there is no king — only equals following known rules, and a crown that passes freely when its holder falls.
What is meant by 'the empty center,' and why would emptiness be a source of usefulness?

Taoist: the hub's hole makes the wheel turn; the room's empty space makes it livable. Emergence: an unfilled center leaves room for parts to organize themselves. Thelemic: a leader who does not fill every role lets others' Will find space. Rights: leaving space is refusing to interfere arbitrarily. Synthesis: usefulness lives in what is left open.

The usefulness of a cup is the emptiness it holds; the usefulness of a wheel is the hollow at its hub around which everything turns. A center that is full of itself has no room for anything else — it blocks the very flow it was meant to enable. In human systems, the empty center is a leader or a structure that deliberately leaves space: unstated details that teams fill with their own judgment, decisions not yet made so that those closer to the work can make them. Consider a good facilitator who says little in a meeting; their restraint is what lets the group's real thinking emerge. Emptiness is not absence of value — it is the condition that makes the surrounding structure work. Fill the center and you jam the wheel.

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Geothermal Power in Iceland

Learn about Iceland's geothermal power and how the Icelandic has harnessed this abundant resource. Discover the best geothermal landmarks and hot springs to visit in Iceland! 
Iceland is known for its many natural wonders which include geothermal attractions such as hot springs, geysers, and landscapes formed by volcanic activity. Geothermal power, derived from the Earth's internal heat, accounts for the majority of Iceland's electricity and heating needs. But why does Iceland have so much of it?
While Iceland may have a reputation as being a chilly place, the entire island and its inhabitants' livelihood has been shaped by the warmth hiding just below the surface. Whether you're washing your hands at your accommodation in Iceland or are taking a self-drive tour to visit the country's best hot springs, geothermal power will play a large role in your visit!
Wh

(1) The Sahara Desert is so dry because it is located in a region where the air is constantly heated and rises, creating a low-pressure area that allows dry air to flow in from the south (source: Why is the Sahara Desert so dry? - Geographical climate, https://www.dwhere.com/why-is-the-sahara-desert-so-dry/).

(2) Iceland has so much geothermal activity because it sits atop the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, where the North American and Eurasian tectonic plates meet, creating abundant geothermal activity and many volcanoes, geysers, and hot springs (source: Geothermal Power in Iceland - Guide to Iceland, https://guidetoiceland.is/best-of-iceland/geothermal-power-in-iceland).

messages.content: A protester blocks a highway to draw attention to a genuine injustice, stranding an ambulance behind the blockade. Where does the line fall?

Taoist: protest that flows around vital arteries persuades; protest that clots them provokes and endangers. Thelemic: the protester's Will to speak is sovereign until it infringes another's Will to live, which the trapped ambulance embodies. Rights: free expression is protected, but the patient's right to life is not a bargaining chip. Emergence: expression channels that disrupt attention without endangering bodies sustain a healthy polity; blockades that risk death invite backlash and repression. Synthesis: defend the protest, condemn the specific harm, and require passage for life.

The right to protest is real and the injustice may be genuine, but a star's Will to be heard stops precisely where it traps another's Will to survive. Blocking the ambulance crosses that line, and no cause is served by a death on the asphalt. The sovereign resolution is not to crush the protest but to bound it: expression may inconvenience and disrupt attention, yet it must yield an open lane for emergencies and life. Defend fiercely their right to shout the injustice from that highway; insist just as fiercely that the ambulance passes. A movement that guards the vulnerable it does not mean to harm keeps the moral high ground its cause requires.

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
History of the web browser

A web browser is a software application for retrieving, presenting and traversing information resources on the World Wide Web. It further provides for the capture or input of information which may be returned to the presenting system, then stored or processed as necessary. The method of accessing a particular page or content is achieved by entering its address, known as a Uniform Resource Identifier or URI. This may be a web page, image, video, or other piece of content.[1] Hyperlinks present in resources enable users easily to navigate their browsers to related resources.
Precursors to the web browser emerged in the form of hyperlinked applications during the mid and late 1980s, and following these, Tim Berners-Lee is credited with developing, in 1990, both the first web server, and the first web browser, called WorldWideWeb (no spaces) and later renamed Nexus.[2] Many others were soon developed, with Marc Andreessen's 1993 Mosaic (later Netscape),[3] being particularly easy to use and install, and often credited with sparking the internet boom of the 1990s.[4] Today, the major web browsers are Chrome, Safari, Firefox, Opera, and Edge.[5]
The explosion in popularity of the Web was triggered in September 1993 by NCSA Mosaic, a graphical browser which eventually ran on several popular office and home computers.[6] This was the first web browser aiming to bring multimedia content to non-technical users, and therefore included images and text on the same page, unlike previous browser designs;[7] its founder, Marc Andreessen, also established the company that in 1994, released Netscape Navigator, which resulted in one of the early browser wars, when it ended up in a competition for dominance (which it lost) with Microsoft's Internet Explorer (for

The first widely used web browser was Mosaic, developed in 1993 by Marc Andreessen and others. It was particularly easy to use and install, and often credited with sparking the internet boom of the 1990s (source: History of the web browser, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_the_web_browser).

- $ -1.11684396980704 $
- $ 16.1168439698070 $
- $ 0 $

function.name: calculate-server-calculate_expression
function.description:
calculate mathematical expressions using the `sympify` function from `sympy`, parse and compute the input mathematical expression string, supports direct calls to SymPy functions (automatically recognizes x, y, z as symbolic variables)
Parameters:
    expression (str): Mathematical expression, e.g., "223 - 344 * 6" or "sin(pi/2) + log(10)".Replace special symbols with approximate values, e.g., pi → 3.1415"
Example expressions:
    "2 + 3*5"                          # Basic arithmetic → 17
    "expand((x + 1)**2)"               # Expand → x² + 2x + 1
    "diff(sin(x), x)"                  # Derivative → cos(x)
    "integrate(exp(x), (x, 0, 1))"      # Definite integral → E - 1
    "solve(x**2 - 4, x)"               # Solve equation → [-2, 2]
    "limit(tan(x)/x, x, 0)"            # Limit → 1
    "Sum(k, (k, 1, 10)).doit()"        # Summation → 55
    "Matrix([[1, 2], [3, 4]]).inv()"   # Matrix inverse → [[-2, 1], [3/2, -1/2]]
    "simplify((x**2 - 1)/(x + 1))"     # Simplify → x - 1
    "factor(x**2 - 2*x - 15)"          # Factorize → (x - 5)(x + 3)
    "series(cos(x), x, 0, 4)"          # Taylor series → 1 - x²/2 + x⁴/24 + O(x⁴)
    "integrate(exp(-x**2)*sin(x), (x, -oo, oo))"  # Complex integral
    "solve([x**2 + y**2 - 1, x + y - 1], [x, y])"  # Solve system of equations
    "Matrix([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6], [7, 8, 9]]).eigenvals()"  # Matrix eigenvalues
Returns:
    str: Calculation result. If the expression cannot be parsed or computed, returns an error message (str).

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
What are mRNA vaccines and how do they work?: MedlinePlus Genetics

Vaccines help prevent infection by preparing the body to fight foreign invaders (such as bacteria, viruses, or other pathogens). All vaccines introduce into the body a harmless piece of a particular bacteria or virus, triggering an immune response. Most vaccines contain a weakened or dead bacteria or virus. However, scientists have developed a new type of vaccine that uses a molecule called messenger RNA (mRNA) rather than part of an actual bacteria or virus. Messenger RNA is a type of RNA that is necessary for protein production.  Once cells finish making a protein, they quickly break down the mRNA. mRNA from vaccines does not enter the nucleus and does not alter DNA.
mRNA vaccines work by introducing a piece of mRNA that corresponds to a viral protein, usually a small piece of a protein found on the virus’s outer membrane. (Individuals who get an mRNA vaccine are not exposed to the virus, nor can they become infected with the virus by the vaccine.) By using this mRNA, cells can produce the viral protein. As part of a normal immune response, the immune system recognizes that the protein is foreign and produces specialized proteins called antibodies. Antibodies help protect the body against infection by recognizing individual viruses or other pathogens, attaching to them, and marking the pathogens for destruction. Once produced, antibodies remain in the body, even after the body has rid itself of the pathogen, so that the immune system can quickly respond if exposed again. If a person is exposed to a virus after receiving mRNA vaccination for it, antibodies can quickly recognize it, attach to it, and mark it for destruction before it can cause serious illness.
Like all vaccines in the United States, mRN

mRNA vaccines work by introducing a piece of mRNA that corresponds to a viral protein, usually a small piece of a protein found on the virus’s outer membrane. Once inside the body, the mRNA instructs cells to produce the viral protein, which triggers an immune response. The immune system then recognizes the protein as foreign and produces antibodies to protect against future infections (source: What are mRNA vaccines and how do they work? - MedlinePlus, https://medlineplus.gov/genetics/understanding/therapy/mrnavaccines/).

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Solar core

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
 This article is about the core of the Sun. For the core of stars in general, see Stellar core.
Cut-away illustration of the Sun showing the core region at center.
The solar core is the hot, dense region at the center of the Sun where energy is generated by nuclear fusion. It is considered to extend from the Sun's center to about 0.2 of the solar radius (139,000 km; 86,000 mi).[1] It is the hottest part of the Sun and of the Solar System. It has a density of 150,000 kg/m3 (150 g/cm3) at the center, and a temperature of 15,000,000 K (27,000,000 °F; 15,000,000 °C).[2]
The core is made of hot, dense plasma (ions and electrons), at a pressure estimated at 26.5 million gigapascals (3.84×1012 psi) at the center.[3] Due to fusion, the composition of the solar plasma drops from about 70% hydrogen by mass at the outer core, to 34% hydrogen at the center.[4]
The core contains 34% of the Sun's mass, but only 3% of the Sun's volume, and it generates 99% of the fusion power of the Sun. There are two distinct reactions in which four hydrogen nuclei may eventually result in one helium nucleus: the proton–proton chain reaction – which is responsible for most of the Sun's released energy – and the CNO cycle.
The composition of the Sun varies with depth.[5]
In the photosphere, it is about 73–74% hydrogen by mass, the rest being primarily helium, which is the same composition as the atmosphere of Jupiter, and the primordial composition of gases at the earliest star formation after the Big Bang. However, as depth into the Sun increases, fusion decreases the fraction of hydrogen. Traveling inward, hydrogen mass fraction starts to decrease rapidly after the core radius has been reached (it is still about 70% at a radius equal to 25% of the Su
The core of the Sun has a temperature of 15,000,000 K (27,000,000 °F; 15,000,000 °C) (source: Solar core, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solar_core).

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Methods of detecting exoplanets

Number of extrasolar planet discoveries per year through 2022, with colors indicating method of detection:
Methods of detecting exoplanets usually rely on indirect strategies – that is, they do not directly image the planet but deduce its existence from another signal. Any planet is an extremely faint light source compared to its parent star. For example, a star like the Sun is about a billion times as bright as the reflected light from any of the planets orbiting it. In addition to the intrinsic difficulty of detecting such a faint light source, the glare from the parent star washes it out. For those reasons, very few of the exoplanets reported as of June 2025 have been detected directly, with even fewer being resolved from their host star.
Established detection methods[edit]
The following methods have proven successful at least once for discovering a new planet or detecting an already discovered planet:
Radial velocity graph of 18 Delphini b.
A star with a planet will move in its own small orbit in response to the planet's gravity. This leads to variations in the speed with which the star moves toward or away from Earth, i.e. the variations are in the radial velocity of the star with respect to Earth. The radial velocity can be deduced from the displacement in the parent star's spectral lines due to the Doppler effect.[1] The radial-velocity method measures these variations in order to confirm the presence of the planet using the binary mass function.
The speed of the star around the system's center of mass is much smaller than that of the planet, because the radius of its orbit around the center of mass is so small. (For example, the Sun moves by about 13 m/s due to Jupiter, but only about 9 cm/s due to Earth). However, velocity varia

Astronomers detect exoplanets using indirect methods, such as the radial velocity method, which measures variations in the speed of a star due to the gravitational pull of an orbiting planet. This method relies on the Doppler effect, analyzing shifts in the star's spectral lines to deduce the presence of a planet. The radial velocity method is particularly effective for detecting massive planets close to their host stars. (source: Methods of detecting exoplanets, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Methods_of_detecting_exoplanets)

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
The Magic of the Amazon Rainforest: Why It’s the Planet’s Lungs

Annette UyThe Amazon Rainforest is a marvel of nature, often referred to as “The Lungs of the Planet” because of its crucial role in producing oxygen and absorbing carbon dioxide. Spanning across nine countries, it covers approximately 5.5 million square kilometers and hosts an astonishing variety of life. The rainforest is home to about 390 billion individual trees, which collectively belong to 16,000 distinct species. This unparalleled biodiversity encompasses a multitude of animal species, many of which are still being discovered.The Power of Photosynthesis: How the Amazon Produces OxygenJuma River is a river in the Amazon rainforest. Image by Alexey Yakovlev, CC BY-SA 2.0 https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0, via Wikimedia CommonsThe term “lungs of the planet” stems from the Amazon Rainforest’s remarkable capacity for photosynthesis. This process involves plants absorbing carbon dioxide and releasing oxygen as a byproduct, effectively refreshing the Earth’s atmosphere. The Amazon alone produces about 20% of the Earth’s oxygen, making it an indispensable component of global ecological balance. Through the sustenance of countless plant species, this rainforest performs a monumental task in supporting terrestrial life.Carbon Sequestration: A Defense Against Climate ChangeSatellite view of the Amazon rainforest. Image by Alexander Gerst, CC BY-SA 2.0 https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/2.0, via Wikimedia CommonsBeyond oxygen production, the Amazon serves as a massive carbon sink. It sequesters millions of tons of carbon dioxide annually, helping to mitigate the impacts of climate change. The vast expanse of trees and plants captures and stores carbon, preventing it from contributing to the

The Amazon rainforest produces a significant amount of oxygen due to the process of photosynthesis, where plants absorb carbon dioxide and release oxygen as a byproduct (source: The Magic of the Amazon Rainforest: Why It’s the Planet’s Lungs, https://discoverwildscience.com/the-magic-of-the-amazon-rainforest-why-its-the-planets-lungs-5-266154/). This process is facilitated by the vast number of trees and plant species in the rainforest, which collectively contribute to the Earth's oxygen supply.

messages.content: I'm researching historical uses of the term "ubiquitous" in literature, but I don't have a clear understanding of its current definition. Could you provide me with the modern-day definition of "ubiquitous"?

messages.name: dictionary-service-get_definitions
messages.content:
Being everywhere at once: omnipresent.
Appearing to be everywhere at once; being or seeming to be in more than one location at the same time.
Widespread; very prevalent.

The modern-day definition of "ubiquitous" includes the following meanings:

1. Being everywhere at once: omnipresent.
2. Appearing to be everywhere at once; being or seeming to be in more than one location at the same time.
3. Widespread; very prevalent.

This term is often used to describe something that is found or seen everywhere, especially when it is so common that it might feel overwhelming. Let me know if you'd like further assistance with your research!

function.name: dictionary-service-get_definitions
function.description: Get definitions for a word.

messages.content: How do stock buybacks affect share price?

function.name: web_search
function.arguments: {"query":"How do stock buybacks affect share price?"}

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] Boosting Wealth: The Impact of Share Buybacks on Investor Returns
Explore how share buybacks elevate EPS and stock value, impacting financial strategy distinctively from dividends.
https://www.investopedia.com/articles/investing/112013/impact-share-repurchases.asp

[2] Understanding Stock Buybacks: Benefits, Drawbacks, and Strategic Uses
Learn why companies repurchase shares, explore the benefits, risks, and strategies of stock buybacks, and understand how they could impact your investment portfolio.
https://www.investopedia.com/terms/b/buyback.asp

[3] How A Stock Buyback Affects Stock Prices & How To Trade It
A stock buyback occurs when a company purchases its own shares from the stock market. This reduces the number of outstanding shares, potentially increasing the share value. Companies use their profits or borrow money to fund these buybacks. The board of directors usually approves the buyback after evaluating the company's financial health.
https://www.liberatedstocktrader.com/stock-buybacks/

[4] How Stock Buybacks Work | Charles Schwab
Some investors believe they can affect companies' health over the long term by diverting cash away from investments in areas like research and development and employee compensation. It's critical for investors to understand why companies use stock buybacks, as well as the potential benefits and risks of doing so.
https://www.schwab.com/learn/story/how-stock-buybacks-work-and-why-they-matter

[5] What happens to a share price during a buyback?
Because buybacks don't guarantee an immediate cash return to shareholders, they allow more flexibility and generally don't force the share price down like dividends (which reduce equity and cash directly). Buybacks can also act as a signal that the company prefers reinvestment and flexibility—which some investors interpret favourably.
https://fairmontequities.com/what-happens-to-a-share-price-during-a-buyback/

[6] Stock Buybacks and Their Impact on Share Prices
Stock buybacks can have a significant impact on share prices, both in the short and long term. The extent of the impact depends on various factors, including the size of the buyback, market conditions, investor sentiment, and the company's overall financial health.
https://industrywired.com/stock-buybacks-and-their-impact-on-share-prices/

[7] Stock Buybacks Explained: How Share Repurchases Affect Price and ...
Stocks Stock Buybacks Explained: How Share Repurchases Affect Price and Shareholder Value Stock buybacks occur when a company uses its cash to repurchase its own shares from the open market, reducing the total number of shares outstanding. S&P 500...
https://www.tradealgo.com/trading-guides/stocks/stock-buybacks-explained-how-share-repurchases-affect-price-and-shareholder-value

[8] Stock Buyback | Formula + Calculator - Wall Street Prep
Yet, share buybacks can still affect a company's valuation, either positively or negatively, contingent on how the market as a whole perceives the decision. Positive Stock Price Impact → If the market incorrectly under-priced the cash a company owns in the valuation, the buyback can cause a higher share price.
https://www.wallstreetprep.com/knowledge/stock-buyback/

CPU vs. GPU: Powerful Options for Your Computing Needs
Central processing units (CPUs) and graphics processing units (GPUs) are fundamental computing engines. But as computing demands evolve, it’s not always clear what their differences are; additionally, what about neural processing units (NPUs) for accelerated AI?
When to Rely on a CPU and When to Choose a GPU
CPUs and GPUs each have unique strengths for your future computing needs.
GPUs are an important part of Intel’s current product lines for integrated (on a CPU) and discrete graphics.
Processors that incorporate the GPU and CPU on the same package offer consumers a blend of high performance and extreme energy efficiency for AI-powered experiences, immersive gaming, and creating.
By
From gaming to video editing to productivity to complex AI experiences1, computing systems today are being asked to do more than ever. CPUs, GPUs, and NPUs have different roles and unique strengths.
What is a CPU?
Constructed from billions of transistors, the central processing unit (CPU) can have multiple processing cores and is commonly referred to as the “brain” of the computer. It is essential to all modern

CPUs are designed for serial processing, focusing on individual tasks with fewer cores, while GPUs are built for parallel processing, handling many tasks simultaneously with many smaller, more specialized cores. CPUs are suited for tasks where latency or per-core performance is important, such as running databases, while GPUs excel at highly parallel tasks like rendering visuals during gameplay or manipulating video data during content creation (source: CPU vs. GPU: What's the Difference?, https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/products/docs/processors/cpu-vs-gpu.html).

messages.content: We're arguing about whether to write extensive comments in code or let the code be self-documenting. The team is split. How should we think about it?

Taoist: excessive comments are willful over-explanation that drifts from the code and lies; the grain favors code that speaks for itself where it can. Rights: readers have a right to comprehension, which means capturing the why that code cannot express. Emergence: good understanding emerges from clear code plus comments precisely where code falls short, not from a blanket policy either way. Thelemic: the author is sovereign over the craft but owes future readers the intent behind non-obvious choices. Synthesis: reject the binary; make code self-documenting for the 'what' and 'how', reserve comments for the 'why' code can't express, and treat comments that merely restate code as noise that decays.

This is a false split, and both camps are defending a half-truth. The 'self-documenting code' camp is right that comments explaining what the code does are usually noise: they restate what a good name or a clear structure already says, they drift out of sync with the code the moment it changes, and a comment that lies is worse than no comment at all. So make the code carry the 'what' and the 'how' through good names, small functions, and clear structure, and delete comments that merely narrate it. But the 'extensive comments' camp is defending something real that the other side misses: code can show what it does and never explain why it does it that way, and the why is exactly what future readers, including the author in six months, most need and can't reconstruct. Why this unusual approach, what constraint forced this ugly workaround, what invariant must not be broken, why the obvious simpler thing doesn't work here, that intent lives only in comments, because the code can't express it. So the rule isn't 'more comments' or 'fewer comments'; it's comment the why, not the what. Let the code document itself wherever it can, and spend comments precisely where understanding requires knowledge that the code, by its nature, cannot hold.
How do coral reefs form?

messages.content:
[1] Corals Tutorial: How Do Coral Reefs Form? - NOAA's National Ocean Service
Learn how coral reefs form from free-swimming larvae attaching to hard surfaces, and how they can be fringing, barrier, or atoll reefs. Explore the different zones of coral reefs and their biogeographic profiles, and the threats and conservation efforts for these diverse ecosystems.
https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/education/tutorial_corals/coral04_reefs.html

[2] How Are Coral Reefs Formed - ThoughtCo
Coral reefs are composed of animals called stony corals. Learn how reefs form and about the types of coral reefs.

[3] Corals Tutorial: How a coral atoll forms - NOAA's National Ocean Service
How a coral atoll forms This animation shows the dynamic process of how a coral atoll forms. Corals (represented in tan and purple) begin to settle and grow around an oceanic island forming a fringing reef. It can take as long as 10,000 years for a fringing reef to form.
https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/education/tutorial_corals/media/supp_coral04a.html

[4] How Do Coral Reefs Form? Coral Polyps, Zooxanthellae And Atolls
Coral reefs form when tiny coral polyps — invertebrate animals partnered with photosynthetic zooxanthellae algae — secrete calcium carbonate skeletons in shallow tropical waters and stack them over centuries into fringing reefs, barrier reefs, and atolls.
https://www.scienceabc.com/nature/how-do-coral-reefs-form

[5] How Are Coral Reefs Formed? A Complete Guide
Discover how coral reefs form through the fascinating process of coral polyp growth, symbiotic relationships, and geological changes that create these ocean wonders.
https://coralvita.co/coral-cafe/how-are-coral-reefs-formed/

[6] Coral reef | Description, Geochemistry, Origins, & Threats | Britannica
Coral reef, ridge or hummock formed in shallow ocean areas by algae and the calcareous skeletons of coral polyps and other coelenterates. A coral reef may grow into a permanent coral island. Often called 'rainforests of the sea,' coral reefs are home to a spectacular variety of organisms.
https://www.britannica.com/science/coral-reef

[7] How Are Coral Reefs Formed? - Coral Reef Information
How are coral reefs formed? Coral reef structure often seem infinitely variable, particularly from the relatively small scale perspective of a person observing reef environments at close range, as would a scuba diver or snorkeler. Yet many or most coral reefs within the larger regions of coral reef development share a surprising degree of similarity in terms of basic reef types and ecological ...
https://www.coral-reef-info.com/how-are-coral-reefs-formed/

[8] Coral reef - Wikipedia
Coral reefs first appeared 485 million years ago, at the dawn of the Early Ordovician, displacing the microbial and sponge reefs of the Cambrian. [2] Sometimes called rainforests of the sea, [3] shallow coral reefs form some of Earth's most diverse ecosystems. They occupy less than 0.1% of the world's ocean area, about half the area of France.
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coral_reef

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Coastal Pollution Tutorial: NOAA's National Ocean Service Education

Coral reefs are created by hard, or stony, coral. Soft corals don’t form reefs, but are often found on reefs, adding essential structural complexity to the entire reef ecosystem.
Coral reefs begin to form when free-swimming coral larvae attach to submerged rocks or other hard surfaces along the edges of islands or continents. As the corals grow and expand, reefs take on one of three major characteristic structures—fringing, barrier, or atoll. Fringing reefs, which are the most common, project seaward directly from the shore, forming borders along the shoreline and surrounding islands. Barrier reefs also border shorelines, but at a greater distance. They are separated from their adjacent land mass by a lagoon of open, often deep water. If a fringing reef forms around a volcanic island that sinks completely below sea level while the coral continues to grow upward, an atoll forms. Atolls are usually circular or oval, with a central lagoon. Parts of the reef platform may emerge as one or more islands, and gaps in the reef provide access to the central lagoon.
In addition to being some of the most beautiful and biologically diverse habitats in the ocean, barrier reefs and atolls also are some of the oldest. With growth rates of 0.3 to 2 centimeters per year for massive corals, and up to 10 centimeters per year for branching corals, it can take up to 10,000 years for a coral reef to form. Depending on their size, barrier reefs and atolls can take from 100,000 to 30 million years to fully form.
All three reef types — fringing, barrier, and atoll — share similarities in their biogeographic profiles. The structure and makeup of the ocean floor in each reef zone, as well as depth, wave and current strength, l

Coral reefs form when free-swimming coral larvae attach to submerged rocks or other hard surfaces along the edges of islands or continents (source: Corals Tutorial: How Do Coral Reefs Form?, https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/education/tutorial_corals/coral04_reefs.html). As the corals grow and expand, they take on one of three major structures—fringing, barrier, or atoll. Fringing reefs are the most common and project seaward directly from the shore, forming borders along the shoreline and surrounding islands.
