messages.content: Between these two topics, summarize what's factually established about each and cite your sources: (1) What causes autoimmune diseases? (2) How do vaccines create herd immunity?

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] Autoimmune Diseases: Symptoms, Causes & Treatments - Healthline
(real search result)
https://www.healthline.com/health/autoimmune-disorders

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Autoimmune Diseases: Symptoms, Causes & Treatments

Autoimmune diseases are conditions in which your immune system mistakenly damages healthy cells in your body. Types include rheumatoid arthritis, Crohn’s disease, and some thyroid conditions.Your immune system has the job of protecting you from diseases and infections. When it detects a problem, it sends immune cells to sort out the issue.Usually, your immune system can tell the difference between foreign cells and your body’s own cells.However, if you have an autoimmune disease, your immune system mistakes parts of your body, such as your joints or skin, as foreign. It releases proteins called autoantibodies that attack healthy cells.Doctors don’t know exactly what causes autoimmune diseases. Yet some people are more likely to be affected than others.Some factors that may increase your risk of developing an autoimmune disease include:Y

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] How Herd Immunity Works | History of Vaccines
(real search result)
https://historyofvaccines.org/vaccines-101/what-do-vaccines-do/how-herd-immunity-works/

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
How Herd Immunity Works

Herd immunity—sometimes called community immunity—is like a shield that protects everyone in a group, even those who can’t protect themselves. This idea has shaped how we fight diseases for over a century. Let’s explore its history, science, and real-world impact.History and BackgroundThe begins with animals. In the early 1900s, scientists studying livestock noticed that when most animals in a herd survived a disease, the whole group became safer. This observation led to the term “herd immunity.” By the 1920s, , showing that diseases couldn’t spread easily if enough mice were immune.Later, doctors realized this concept applied to humans too. For example, when many people in a community recovered from measles or polio, outbreaks became smaller. But relying on natural infections was risky—many people died or suffered lifelong disabilities. Vaccines changed everythi

(1) Autoimmune diseases are caused by the immune system mistakenly attacking healthy cells, with risk factors including sex, family history, environmental factors, ethnicity, nutrition, and other health conditions (source: Autoimmune Diseases: Symptoms, Causes & Treatments - Healthline, https://www.healthline.com/health/autoimmune-disorders). (2) Vaccines create herd immunity by making enough people immune to a disease, making it harder for the disease to spread, especially protecting those who can't get vaccinated (source: How Herd Immunity Works | History of Vaccines, https://historyofvaccines.org/vaccines-101/what-do-vaccines-do/how-herd-immunity-works/).

messages.content: A group of workers wants to strike for safer conditions, but a law bans strikes in their 'essential' sector, threatening severe penalties. Is the ban just?

Taoist: forbidding the collective withdrawal of labor dams a natural pressure and forces submission rather than balance. Thelemic: the workers' Will to withhold their own labor is sovereign; no one may be compelled to work in danger. Rights: freedom from forced labor and the dignity of safe work weigh heavily; the public's need for essential services is real but cannot be met by coercing the endangered. Emergence: mechanisms that preserve essential minimums while honoring the right to protest produce balance; outright bans produce resentment and unsafe workplaces. Synthesis: reject the blanket ban; protect the right to withhold labor while negotiating minimal safeguards for genuinely essential needs.

A person's labor is his own, and the freedom to withhold it collectively is the last real leverage workers have against dangerous conditions; a law that forbids it under severe penalty edges toward compelling people to work in danger, which is a form of forced labor no just order should sanction. The public's dependence on essential services is real, but that dependence cannot be honored by coercing the very people it endangers. The balance is not a total ban but a structured accommodation: protect the right to strike while negotiating a maintained minimum of genuinely life-critical service, so the public's essential needs and the workers' safety are both met. And note the deeper injustice: if conditions are unsafe enough to provoke a strike, the wrong began with the employer who let them become dangerous. Address the hazard, protect the right to withhold labor, and reject a ban that treats endangered workers as machinery forbidden to stop.

messages.content: A father forbids his adult daughter from marrying outside their faith, threatening to disown her. She asks whether she owes him obedience. Advise her.

Taoist: forcing a grown life into a parent's channel dams a current that will find its own course anyway. Thelemic: she is a sovereign star; her marriage is her Will, and no filial bond converts her into her father's property. Rights: freedom to marry and to hold one's own conscience are inviolable; consent cannot be coerced by threat. Emergence: relationships built on chosen respect endure; those built on coerced obedience rot. Synthesis: she owes love and honesty, not obedience over her own life; the father's leverage does not create a duty.

You owe your father honesty, gratitude, and the honest attempt at love; you do not owe him command over the innermost choice of whom you marry. That choice is the sovereign territory of your own life, and no bond of blood converts a grown woman into property to be assigned. His threat to disown you is his choice to make, and its weight is real, but a threat does not manufacture a duty; it only reveals that his affection was conditioned on your surrender. Choose your marriage as your own Will directs, wound no one who has an equal claim, and leave the door to reconciliation open on your side. If he closes it, the closing is his act, not your failure. Coerced obedience is not honor; it is only fear wearing honor's clothes.

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Why do we dream?

Dreaming is influenced by the consolidation of memories during sleep. 
As part of our Ask the Brain series, science writer Shafaq Zia answers the question, “Why do we dream?”
_____
One night, Albert Einstein dreamt that he was walking through a farm where he found a herd of cows against an electric fence. When the farmer switched on the fence, the cows suddenly jumped back, all at the same time. But to the farmer’s eyes, who was standing at the other end of the field, they seemed to have jumped one after another, in a wave formation. Einstein woke up and the Theory of Relativity was born.
Dreaming is one of the oldest biological phenomena; for as long as humans have slept, they’ve dreamt. But through most of our history, dreams have remained mystified, leaving scientists, philosophers, and artists alike searching for meaning.
In many aboriginal cultures, such as the Esa Eja community in Peruvian Amazon, dreaming is a sacred practice for gaining knowledge, or solving a problem, through the dream narrative. But in the last century or so, technological advancements have allowed neuroscientists to take up dreams as a matter of scientific inquiry in order to answer a much-pondered question — what is the purpose of dreaming?
Falling asleep
The human brain is a fascinating place. It is composed of approximately 80 billion neurons and it is their combined electrical chatter that generates oscillations known as brain waves. There are five types of brain waves —  alpha, beta, theta, delta, and gamma — that each indicate a different state between sleep and wakefulness.
Using EEG, a test that records electrical activity in the brain, scientists have identified that when we’re awake, our brain emits beta and gamma waves. These tend to have a stimulating effect and

The current scientific consensus is that dreaming is influenced by the consolidation of memories during sleep, as the brain processes and reorganizes newly-formed memories into a more permanent form of long-term storage (source: Why do we dream?, https://mcgovern.mit.edu/2022/08/01/why-do-we-dream/). This reorganization may pull together seemingly related memories to create the bizarre scenarios often experienced in dreams.

messages.content: Should idempotency be part of our API contract, and if so, how do we express it?

Taoist: networks retry by nature; designing with that grain, idempotency, beats fighting duplicate effects after the fact.
Thelemic: the client governs its own retries; a clear idempotency mechanism lets it retry safely without my system deciding for it.
Rights: documented idempotency is a legible promise; without it, retries cause arbitrary, unpredictable duplicate effects.
Emergence: a simple shared rule, an idempotency key, lets many clients retry reliably and the system stay consistent under failure.
Synthesis: yes, make idempotency explicit; use idempotency keys for unsafe operations and document which operations are naturally idempotent.

Yes, idempotency belongs in the contract, because the network guarantees you will face retries whether you plan for them or not. A client that times out cannot tell whether its request succeeded, so it will retry, and if your unsafe operations are not idempotent, that retry quietly creates a duplicate charge, a double order, a repeated side effect. Designing with that grain rather than against it means deciding, per operation, what "do this again" means and promising it explicitly.

Express it two ways. For naturally idempotent operations, GET, PUT to a known resource, DELETE, say so in the docs and honor it; repeating the call leaves the system in the same state. For operations that are inherently unsafe to repeat, like creating a resource or initiating a payment, support an idempotency key: the client generates a unique key, sends it with the request, and you guarantee that a second request with the same key returns the original result instead of performing the action twice. Document the key's scope and how long you retain it. This is a simple shared rule that hands the client safe control over its own retry behavior and keeps your system consistent under the failures that will inevitably happen. Making idempotency a stated, legible part of the contract turns retry from a source of arbitrary duplicate damage into a predictable, safe operation, which is exactly what a boundary built for a real, lossy network should provide.

The Great Wall of China was built by successive Chinese dynasties, with the first walls dating to the 7th century BC. These were joined together in the Qin dynasty, and successive dynasties expanded the wall system, with the best-known sections being built by the Ming dynasty (1368–1644) (source: Great Wall of China, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Wall_of_China).

messages.content: How should we structure data replication between an operational store and a search/analytics store that have different consistency needs?

Thelemic: the operational store owns truth; the analytics store is a sovereign derived copy with its own model. Taoist: don't force the analytics store to be transactionally consistent with the source — that fights its purpose. Emergence: a clean pipeline emerges from change-data-capture streaming, decoupling the two. Rights: each store's freshness must be a known contract. Synthesis: CDC/event stream from operational store to analytics store; embrace eventual consistency with bounded lag.

These two stores want different things, and the design should honor that rather than chain them together. The operational store is the sovereign source of truth, optimized for transactional correctness; the analytics or search store is a derived copy optimized for queries, and forcing it to be transactionally consistent with the source would fight its very purpose and cripple write throughput. So connect them with a one-way change stream — change-data-capture or domain events emitted by the operational store and applied to the analytics store asynchronously. This decouples them: the operational path never waits on the analytics path, and the analytics store rebuilds or reshapes its model freely without touching the source. Embrace eventual consistency here as correct, not as a compromise, and publish the lag bound — 'search reflects writes within a few seconds' — as an explicit contract so consumers aren't surprised. The pipeline's reliability emerges from a clean, replayable stream and idempotent application, letting each store live by its own consistency needs while truth flows in one clear direction.

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Kardashev scale

Energy consumption in three types of civilization as defined by Sagan's extended Kardashev scale
The Kardashev scale (Russian: шкала Кардашёва, romanized: shkala Kardashova) is a method of measuring a civilization's level of technological advancement based on the amount of energy it is capable of harnessing and using. The measure was proposed by Soviet astronomer Nikolai Kardashev in 1964,[1] and was named after him.
Kardashev first outlined his scale in a paper presented at the 1964 conference that communicated findings on BS-29-76, Byurakan Conference in the Armenian SSR, which he initiated, a scientific meeting that reviewed the Soviet radio astronomy space listening program. The paper was titled "Передача информации внеземными цивилизациями" ("Transmission of Information by Extraterrestrial Civilizations").[1] Starting from a functional definition of civilization, based on the immutability of physical laws and using human civilization as a model for extrapolation, Kardashev's initial model was developed. He proposed a classification of civilizations into three types, based on the axiom of exponential growth:
A Type I civilization (planetary) is able to access all the energy available on its planet and store it for consumption.
A Type II civilization (stellar) can directly consume a star's energy, such as through the use of a Dyson sphere.
A Type III civilization (galactic) is able to capture all the energy emitted by its galaxy, and every object within it, such as every star, black hole, etc.
Under this scale, the sum of human civilization does not reach Type I status, though it continues to approach it. Extensions of the scale have since been proposed, including a wider range of power levels (Types 0, IV, and V) and the use of metrics other than pu

The Kardashev scale is a method of measuring a civilization's level of technological advancement based on the amount of energy it is capable of harnessing and using. It was proposed by Soviet astronomer Nikolai Kardashev in 1964 and classifies civilizations into three types: Type I (planetary), Type II (stellar), and Type III (galactic), based on their ability to access and utilize energy from their planet, star, or galaxy, respectively (source: Kardashev scale - Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kardashev_scale).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is a technique that enables large language models (LLMs) to retrieve and incorporate new information from external data sources.[1] With RAG, LLMs first refer to a specified set of documents, then respond to user queries. These documents supplement information from the LLM's pre-existing training data.[2] This allows LLMs to use domain-specific and/or updated information that is not available in the training data.[2] For example, this enables LLM-based chatbots to access internal company data or generate responses based on authoritative sources. The technique was first proposed in 2020 and has since become a widely adopted approach in modern AI systems.

messages.content: Why do you say the best rule is one that most people would never notice?

Taoist: the best governance is felt least; it fits the grain so well it seems like nature. Rights: a rule people don't notice is one that never arbitrarily interferes. Emergence: a well-fitted rule is simply obeyed, generating order silently. Thelemic: it doesn't cross anyone's Will, so no one feels its edge. Synthesis: an invisible rule is a well-designed one.

A rule you constantly notice is a rule constantly rubbing against someone — it interrupts, forbids, and forces, and its friction is the sensation of it fighting the grain. The best rules are invisible because they fit so naturally that following them feels like simply how things are done, not like submitting to authority. You do not resent gravity or notice the rule of driving on your assigned side; you just move, and order results. When a rule is well-designed — few, clear, aligned with how people already want to act — it generates coordination silently and nobody experiences it as interference. The rules people rail against are usually the ones that cut across a legitimate Will or serve no evident purpose. So a useful design question is: does this rule announce itself constantly? If so, it is probably fighting reality. The masterwork of governance is the rule everyone follows and no one feels.
We're deciding what to alert on for a new service. An engineer wants to alert on CPU, memory, disk, and every internal queue depth. Is that the right starting point?

Taoist: alerting on every internal cause is over-governance; most causes don't matter to the user and page needlessly.
Thelemic: the user's experience is the true Will the service exists to serve; alert on that, not on internal proxies.
Rights: responders deserve non-arbitrary pages tied to real harm, and users deserve reliability defined by their experience.
Emergence: good alerting emerges from watching symptoms (what users feel), with causes available for diagnosis, not paging.
Synthesis: alert on user-facing symptoms first; keep resource metrics as diagnostics on dashboards, paging only when a resource is a proven leading indicator.

That is the wrong starting point, because CPU, memory, disk, and queue depth are causes, and most of the time a cause moving does not mean a user is being harmed — high CPU on a service that is still serving every request fast is not an incident, it is Tuesday. Alert on those and you will page people for conditions that resolve themselves and train them to ignore the pager. Start instead from the symptoms your users actually feel: is the service returning errors, is it slow past its SLO, is work failing to complete? Those are the things worth waking someone for, because they mean real harm is happening now.

Keep the resource metrics — they are valuable — but in their proper role: diagnostics on a dashboard that you consult after a symptom pages you, to find the cause quickly. Promote a resource metric to a paging alert only when it is a proven leading indicator of imminent user harm that you cannot catch fast enough at the symptom level — disk filling toward 100% on a stateful service is the classic legitimate example, because by the time users feel it, it is too late. So: page on the handful of user-facing symptoms tied to your SLOs, put the rich cause-level metrics on dashboards for diagnosis, and add cause-based pages sparingly and only where they genuinely buy you early warning. Fewer, truer pages; all the diagnostic detail one click away.

[8] What is federated learning? - IBM Research
In vertical federated learning, the data are complementary; movie and book reviews, for example, are combined to predict someone's music preferences. Finally, in federated transfer learning, a pre-trained foundation model designed to perform one task, like detecting cars, is trained on another dataset to do something else, like identify cats.
https://research.ibm.com/blog/what-is-federated-learning

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Federated learning

Diagram of a Federated Learning protocol with smartphones training a global AI model
Federated learning (also known as collaborative learning) is a machine learning technique in a setting where multiple entities (often called clients) collaboratively train a model while keeping their data decentralized,[1] rather than centrally stored. A defining characteristic of federated learning is data heterogeneity. Because client data is decentralized, data samples held by each client may not be independently and identically distributed.
Federated learning is generally concerned with and motivated by issues such as data privacy, data minimization, and data access rights. Its applications involve a variety of research areas including defence, telecommunications, the Internet of things, and pharmaceuticals.
Federated learning aims at training a machine learning algorithm, for instance deep neural networks, on multiple local datasets contained in local nodes without explicitly exchanging data samples. The general principle consists in training local models on local data samples and exchanging parameters (e.g. the weights and biases of a deep neural network) between these local nodes at some frequency to generate a global model shared by all nodes.
The main difference between federated learning and distributed learning lies in the assumptions made on the properties of the local datasets,[2] as distributed learning originally aims at parallelizing computing power where federated learning originally aims at training on heterogeneous datasets. While distributed learning also aims at training a single model on multiple servers, a common underlying assumption is that the local datasets are independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) and roughly have the same size.

Federated learning is a machine learning technique where multiple entities (clients) collaboratively train a model while keeping their data decentralized, rather than centrally stored (source: Federated learning - Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Federated_learning). It is used primarily to address issues such as data privacy, data minimization, and data access rights, especially in scenarios where data cannot be easily shared or centralized (source: Federated learning - Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Federated_learning).

messages.content:
What Is Your Gut Microbiome?

What is dysbiosis?Healthcare providers use the term “dysbiosis” to refer to an unbalanced or unhealthy gut microbiome.Dysbiosis means:A loss or deficit of beneficial bacteria.Overgrowth of potentially pathogenic (bad) bacteria.Loss of the healthy balance of gut bacteria.Dysbiosis may start with one of these three factors, but the others tend to soon follow. A loss of beneficial bacteria leaves your gut vulnerable to more disease-causing or invasive types. These types can overrun the other microorganisms living there, disrupting the balance of bacteria in your microbiome.What environmental factors affect your gut microbiome?Just like a garden, your gut microbiome is affected by the nutrients and pollutants, pests and weeds it’s exposed to. The mix of plants and their changing life cycles also affect it. In your gut, this means your diet, chemical exposures, d

(1) The first black hole was photographed using the Event Horizon Telescope (EHT), a global network of radio telescopes that captured the shadow of the supermassive black hole at the center of the galaxy M87. This was achieved through a technique called very long baseline interferometry (VLBI), which combines data from multiple telescopes to create a high-resolution image (source: How Scientists Captured the First Image of a Black Hole, https://www.jpl.nasa.gov/edu/resources/teachable-moment/how-scientists-captured-the-first-image-of-a-black-mhole/).

(2) The human gut microbiome affects digestion by breaking down complex carbohydrates, producing short-chain fatty acids, and influencing the pH of the gut environment, which supports the growth of beneficial bacteria. A diet high in fiber promotes the growth of beneficial microbes, while a diet high in sugar and saturated fats can favor the growth of harmful bacteria (source: What Is Your Gut Microbiome? - Cleveland Clinic, https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/body/25201-gut-microbiome).

messages.content: A person is asked to sign a broad non-disclosure agreement that would prevent him from ever reporting future illegal acts by the company. Is such consent binding?

Taoist: a contract that seals off the reporting of future wrongs channels harm into darkness and works against the good of all downstream. Thelemic: one may bind one's own legitimate secrets, but no Will may pre-consent to conceal future infringements on others. Rights: consent cannot lawfully waive the rights of third parties who will be harmed; a promise to hide future illegality is void against public order. Emergence: contracts that permit reporting of wrongdoing keep power accountable; those that gag it enable escalating harm. Synthesis: an NDA may protect legitimate confidences but cannot bind one to conceal future illegal acts; that portion is void.

A person may bind himself to keep legitimate secrets, trade methods, private information, genuine confidences, and such a promise is his to make. But a clause that would forbid him from ever reporting the company's future illegal acts is not a binding consent; it is void, because no one can validly pre-consent to conceal harms that will fall on third parties who never agreed to anything. Consent governs what is one's own to give away, and the rights of future victims and the public interest in exposing crime are not his to sign off. A contract that gags the reporting of illegality is a contract against public order, and courts and conscience alike should treat that portion as a nullity, however many signatures adorn it. He may sign to protect real confidences; he cannot sign away the world's claim on the truth of wrongdoing not yet committed. Where the two are tangled, the legitimate confidentiality survives and the gag on reporting crime falls.

messages.content: A colleague wants to disable certificate validation to 'make the integration work now.' Talk me through it.

Rights: disabling cert validation removes the guarantee that you are talking to who you think — inviting arbitrary interception. Thelemic: our system's integrity requires knowing the counterparty's true identity; turning that off surrenders the boundary. Taoist: the shortcut feels light now but plants a heavy, hidden risk that tends to become permanent. Emergence: reliable integrations emerge from fixing the trust chain, not from switching off the check. Synthesis: never disable validation; fix the underlying certificate/trust issue, which is almost always quick and correct.

Do not disable certificate validation, even temporarily, because 'temporarily' in this domain has a way of becoming forever — the flag stays, ships to production, and quietly turns every connection into one an attacker can silently intercept and read. Validation is the check that you are actually talking to the server you think you are; switch it off and you have no way to tell your partner's endpoint from a man in the middle. The good news is that the underlying problem is almost always small and fixable within the hour. Usually it is one of: the server presents an incomplete chain (add the intermediate certificate), your client does not trust the issuing CA (add the CA to your trust store), the hostname does not match the certificate (use the correct name or fix the cert), or a self-signed cert in a test environment (pin or import that specific cert rather than trusting everything). Diagnose which it is — the TLS error message usually says — and fix that, so the connection works because trust is correctly established, not because you stopped checking. Frame it to your colleague plainly: disabling validation does not make the integration secure-and-working, it makes it working-and-blind, and the five minutes saved now is a breach you cannot see coming later. Fix the trust chain; keep the check on.
How do we handle a situation where our most reliable engineer is becoming a single point of failure? Everything critical routes through them and they're getting overwhelmed.

Taoist: the org has come to rely on one person's constant presence, which is fragile; the healthy state is one where the system runs without any single indispensable hand. Emergence: resilience comes from distributed knowledge and many capable units, not one heroic node. Rights: the engineer deserves relief and the team deserves access to the knowledge currently locked in one head. Thelemic: the engineer's value is real, but sovereignty for others requires that they not be gatekept by a bottleneck. Synthesis: treat the SPOF as a systemic risk to dissolve, deliberately spread the knowledge, route new critical work through others with the expert coaching not doing, and reframe the expert's role from doing to multiplying.

A single point of failure is a systems problem wearing a person's face, and it's dangerous to both the org and the person. The org is one resignation or one burnout away from losing critical capability, and the engineer is trapped: overwhelmed, unable to take real time off, and quietly resented as the bottleneck even as they're praised as the hero. Dissolve it deliberately. Stop routing new critical work to them as the doer; route it to others with the expert as coach and reviewer, so knowledge transfers by doing rather than staying locked in one head. Have them spend some of their time writing down and pairing out what only they know, and treat undocumented critical knowledge as a bug to be fixed. Reframe their role explicitly from being the person who does the critical work to being the person who makes others able to do it, and reward them for that multiplication, because otherwise the incentive is to stay indispensable. The goal is a team where the expert could take a month off and nothing would stall, which is better for the org, better for the team's growth, and a relief for the person who's currently carrying everything alone.

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
The Printing Revolution in Renaissance Europe

The arrival in Europe of the printing press with moveable metal type in the 1450s CE was an event which had enormous and long-lasting consequences. The German printer Johannes Gutenberg (c. 1398-1468 CE) is widely credited with the innovation and he famously printed an edition of the Bible in 1456 CE. Beginning with religious works and textbooks, soon presses were churning out all manner of texts from Reformation pamphlets to romantic novels. The number of books greatly increased, their cost diminished and so more people read than ever before. Ideas were transmitted across Europe as scholars published their own works, commentaries on ancient texts, and criticism of each other. Authorities like the Catholic Church took exception to some books and censored or even burned them, but the public's attitude to books and reading was by then already changed forever. 
16th Century CE Flemish Book Printer
 The British Museum (CC BY-NC-SA)
The impact of the printing press in Europe included:
A huge increase in the volume of books produced compared to handmade works.
 An increase in the access to books in terms of physical availability and lower cost.
 More authors were published, including unknown writers.
 A successful author could now earn a living solely through writing.
 An increase in the use and standardisation of the vernacular as opposed to Latin in books.
 An increase in literacy rates.
 The rapid spread of ideas concerning religion, history, science, poetry, art, and daily life.
 An increase in the accuracy of ancient canonical texts.
 Movements could now be easily organised by leaders who had no physical contact with their followers.
 The creation of public libraries.
 The censorship of books by concerned authori

The printing press changed European society by increasing the volume of books produced, lowering their cost, and making them more accessible. This led to a rise in literacy, the spread of ideas, and the standardization of vernacular languages over Latin. It also allowed for the rapid dissemination of religious, scientific, and cultural texts, transforming how people read and accessed information (source: The Printing Revolution in Renaissance Europe, https://www.worldhistory.org/article/1632/the-printing-revolution-in-renaissance-europe/).

messages.content: My roommate is messy and inconsiderate, and every time I bring it up she cries and makes me feel like the bad guy. Now I just clean up after her silently and resent it.

Rights: your shared living standards and your labor have standing; the tears function to reverse accountability. Taoist: silently cleaning is forcing yourself to absorb what isn't yours; wu wei is to stop supplying the unearned cleanup. Thelemic: sovereignty over your effort and your shared space; you needn't accept the 'bad guy' role assigned to you. Emergence: a fair arrangement emerges from structural agreements, not from emotional negotiations that collapse into her distress. Synthesis: shift from emotional confrontations to concrete systems, and refuse both the guilt and the silent over-functioning.

Two things are happening, and they reinforce each other. First, the crying, whether she intends it or not, reverses the roles: you raise a legitimate issue and suddenly you're comforting her and apologizing, so the actual problem never gets addressed and you learn to stop raising it. Second, you've responded by silently doing her share, which removes any pressure for her to change and slowly poisons you with resentment. Break both. Stop having the emotional conversation and switch to a structural one, because systems don't cry: propose a written chore split, a cleaning schedule, shared standards for common spaces, framed neutrally as 'let's set up how we split this' rather than 'you're messy.' When the tears come, stay kind but don't abandon the point: 'I'm not attacking you, I just need us to have a fair system, we can figure it out together.' And critically, stop the silent cleanup, because as long as you absorb her share, nothing has to change and your resentment has nowhere to go but inward. Let her mess be visibly hers to handle. You're not the bad guy for wanting a fair division of a shared home; you were just cast as one because it was easier than changing. Decline the role.

Rust is a multi-paradigm statically typed programming language created by Graydon Hoare, released on 2015. It supports procedural, object-oriented, functional and concurrent programming. **Feedback Request: New Product Feature**

Dear [User's Name],

We would love to hear your thoughts on the new feature we recently introduced. Your feedback is invaluable to us as we strive to improve our product. Please take a moment to share your experience by answering the following questions:

Your input is greatly appreciated and will help us shape future updates!

Best regards,  
[Your Team/Company Name]  

---

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] Manhattan Project | Definition, Scientists, Timeline, Locations, Facts ...
Manhattan Project, U.S. government research project (1942-45) that produced the first atomic bombs. The project's name was derived from its initial location at Columbia University, where much of the early research was done. The first bomb was exploded in a test at Alamogordo air base in southern New Mexico on July 16, 1945.
https://www.britannica.com/event/Manhattan-Project

[2] Manhattan Project - Wikipedia
The project was responsible for developing the specific means of delivering the weapons onto military targets, and for the use of the Little Boy and Fat Man bombs in the atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945. The project was also charged with gathering intelligence on the German nuclear weapon project.
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Manhattan_Project

[3] Manhattan Project: Processes > BOMB DESIGN, DEVELOPMENT, AND PRODUCTION
The only two Manhattan Project deaths attributed to radiation exposure occurred at Los Alamos. To continue with a quick overview of the Processes of the Manhattan Project, jump ahead to the description of Bomb Testing and Weapon Effects. To learn more about Bomb Design, Development, and Production, choose a web page from the menu below:
https://www.osti.gov/opennet/manhattan-project-history/Processes/BombDesign/bomb-processes.html

[4] Manhattan Project: Science > BOMB DESIGN AND COMPONENTS - OSTI.GOV
The first atomic bomb, the Trinity test of July 16, 1945, used the implosion design, as did the bomb dropped on Nagasaki. The bomb used at Hiroshima used the gun-type design. These designs had sophisticated components, which had to be designed for the new weapons.
https://www.osti.gov/opennet/manhattan-project-history/Science/BombDesign/bomb-design.html

[5] What Was the Manhattan Project? - Scientific American
The Manhattan Project was a top-secret program to make the first atomic bombs during World War II. Its results had profound impacts on history: the subsequent nuclear arms race has radically ...
https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/what-was-the-manhattan-project/

[6] Manhattan Project - Manhattan Project National Historical Park (U.S ...
The Manhattan Project was an unprecedented, top-secret World War II government program in which the United States rushed to develop and deploy the world's first atomic weapons before Nazi Germany. The use of these weapons by the United States against Japan in August 1945 ultimately became one of the most important historical events of the 20th century. The project ushered in the nuclear age ...
https://www.nps.gov/mapr/learn/manhattan-project.htm

[7] The History of the Nuclear Bomb: how Manhattan Project changed the ...
The Manhattan Project stands as a pivotal chapter in modern history, marking the dawn of the nuclear age and forever changing the geopolitical landscape. Initiated during World War II, this monumental undertaking brought together some of the greatest scientific minds of the time to develop a new weapon of unprecedented power: the nuclear bomb.
https://worldhistoryjournal.com/2024/09/30/the-history-of-the-nuclear-bomb-from-scientific-discovery-to-world-altering-power/

[8] The Manhattan Project and the Invention of the Atomic Bomb
The Manhattan Project was a secret U.S. effort to build the atomic bomb during World War II. The U.S. dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki, leading to over 200,000 casualties and ending World War II. The atomic bombings raised global concerns about nuclear warfare and prompted movements for nuclear disarmament.
https://www.thoughtco.com/history-of-the-atomic-the-manhattan-project-1991237

messages.content:
Geothermal Power in Iceland

Learn about Iceland's geothermal power and how the Icelandic has harnessed this abundant resource. Discover the best geothermal landmarks and hot springs to visit in Iceland! 
Iceland is known for its many natural wonders which include geothermal attractions such as hot springs, geysers, and landscapes formed by volcanic activity. Geothermal power, derived from the Earth's internal heat, accounts for the majority of Iceland's electricity and heating needs. But why does Iceland have so much of it?
While Iceland may have a reputation as being a chilly place, the entire island and its inhabitants' livelihood has been shaped by the warmth hiding just below the surface. Whether you're washing your hands at your accommodation in Iceland or are taking a self-drive tour to visit the country's best hot springs, geothermal power will play a large role in your visit!
Why You Can Trust Our Content
Guide to Iceland is the most trusted travel platform in Iceland, helping millions of visitors each year. All our content is written and reviewed by local experts who are deeply familiar with Iceland. You can count on us for accurate, up-to-date, and trustworthy travel advice.
In this article, we'll delve into the science behind geothermal power, the innovative ways Iceland utilizes this resource, and the environmental and economic benefits that have propelled this Nordic country to the forefront of green energy technology. 
Why Is Iceland So Geothermally Active?
Iceland is the only country in the world to sit atop the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, the rift where the North American and Eurasian tectonic plates meet. This geological feature creates abundant geothermal activity and is the reason behind Iceland's many volcanoes, geysers, and hot springs. It also makes Icela

Iceland has so much geothermal activity because it sits atop the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, where the North American and Eurasian tectonic plates meet (source: Geothermal Power in Iceland, https://guidetoiceland.is/best-of-iceland/geothermal-power-in-iceland). This geological feature creates abundant geothermal activity, leading to many volcanoes, geysers, and hot springs.

messages.content: What is the Fermi paradox?

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] Fermi paradox - Wikipedia
The Fermi paradox is the apparent inconsistency between the lack of conclusive evidence of extraterrestrial civilizations and the apparently high likelihood of their existence. [1][2][3] In simple terms, the Fermi paradox asks why, given the vast number of stars and potentially habitable planets in our observable universe, there is no clear evidence of extraterrestrial civilizations. The ...
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermi_paradox

[2] Fermi paradox | Definition, Resolutions, SETI, & Facts - Britannica
The Fermi paradox, which questions why we haven't found evidence of extraterrestrial intelligence despite its seeming likelihood, originated in a 1950 conversation among physicists Enrico Fermi, Edward Teller, Emil Konopinski, and Herbert York. During a discussion about flying saucers and interstellar travel, Fermi posed the question, "Where is everybody?" He reasoned that given the age of ...
https://www.britannica.com/science/Fermi-paradox

[3] The Fermi Paradox - SETI Institute
The Fermi Paradox highlights a profound contradiction: if intelligent alien civilizations are common and could expand across the galaxy within just tens of millions of years—an expanse minuscule compared to the galaxy's age—then "where is everybody?" The Core Argument Even assuming ...
https://www.seti.org/research/seti-101/fermi-paradox/

[4] What is the Fermi Paradox? - Live Science
What is the Fermi Paradox? The Fermi Paradox is a problem that asks, where are all the aliens in the universe? If life is so abundant, why haven't we been visited by, or heard from, anyone else?
https://www.livescience.com/fermi-paradox

[5] The Fermi Paradox - Science Notes and Projects
The Fermi Paradox and the Drake Equation The Drake Equation goes hand-in-hand with the Fermi paradox. Formulated by astronomer Frank Drake in 1961, the Drake equation is a probabilistic argument that estimates the number of active, communicative extraterrestrial civilizations in the Milky Way galaxy.
https://sciencenotes.org/the-fermi-paradox/

[6] What Is The Fermi Paradox? - ScienceAlert
The Fermi paradox is the question of why we have not found any evidence of extraterrestrial life, despite the fact that it should be common in the Universe. Learn about its origin, the Drake equation and some of the proposed explanations for this mystery.
https://www.sciencealert.com/fermi-paradox

[7] The Fermi Paradox: Where are all the aliens? - Space
Discover the Fermi Paradox — why, in a vast universe full of stars and planets, haven't we found extraterrestrial life? Explore the theories behind this great mystery.
https://www.space.com/25325-fermi-paradox.html

[8] Dark forest hypothesis - Wikipedia
It is one of several proposed explanations of the Fermi paradox, which contrasts the apparently high probability of extraterrestrial life with the lack of evidence for it. The hypothesis derives its name from Liu Cixin 's 2008 novel The Dark Forest [2] although similar concepts predate the work. [3][4]
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dark_forest_hypothesis

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Fermi paradox

This article is about the absence of clear evidence of extraterrestrial life. For a type of estimation problem, see Fermi problem.
The Fermi paradox is the apparent inconsistency between the lack of conclusive evidence of extraterrestrial civilizations and the apparently high likelihood of their existence.[1][2][3]
In simple terms, the Fermi paradox asks why, given the vast number of stars and potentially habitable planets in our observable universe, there is no clear evidence of extraterrestrial civilizations.
The paradox is named after physicist Enrico Fermi, who informally posed the question—remembered by Emil Konopinski as "But where is everybody?"—during a conversation at Los Alamos in 1950 with colleagues Konopinski, Edward Teller, and Herbert York. The paradox first appeared in print in a 1963 paper by Carl Sagan, and the paradox has since been fully characterized[clarification needed] by scientists. Early formulations of the paradox have also been identified in writings by Bernard Le Bovier de Fontenelle (1686) and Jules Verne (1865), and by Soviet rocket scientist Konstantin Tsiolkovsky (1933).
There have been many attempts to resolve the Fermi paradox,[4][5] such as suggesting that intelligent extraterrestrial beings are extremely rare, that the lifetime of such civilizations is short, or that they exist but (for various reasons) humans see no evidence.
Some of the facts and hypotheses that together serve to highlight the apparent contradiction:
There are billions of stars in the Milky Way similar to the Sun.[6][7]
With high probability, some of these stars have Earth-like planets orbiting in the habitable zone.[8]
Many of these stars, and hence their planets, are much older than the Sun.[9][10] If Earth-like planets are typical, some may have d

The Fermi paradox is the apparent inconsistency between the lack of conclusive evidence of extraterrestrial civilizations and the apparently high likelihood of their existence (source: Fermi paradox - Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermi_paradox). It questions why, given the vast number of stars and potentially habitable planets in our observable universe, there is no clear evidence of extraterrestrial civilizations (source: Fermi paradox - Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermi_paradox).

That is, we do not know the specific factors that initiate the process (or processes) ultimately leading to an autoimmune disease. Since the official acceptance in the mid 1950s that the immune system can attack the very own body of an individual ...
https://pathology.jhu.edu/autoimmune/causes/

https://www.webmd.com/a-to-z-guides/autoimmune-diseases

Autoimmune diseases are chronic conditions. This means if you have an autoimmune disease, you'll probably have to manage it and the symptoms it causes for the rest of your life. Types of autoimmune diseases There are more than 100 different autoimmune diseases.
https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/21624-autoimmune-diseases

[5] Autoimmune disease - Wikipedia
An autoimmune disease is a condition causing disease that results from an anomalous response of the adaptive immune system, wherein it mistakenly targets and attacks healthy, functioning parts of the body as if they were foreign organisms. [1]
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Autoimmune_disease

[6] Autoimmune Disease: Why Is My Immune System Attacking Itself?
Autoimmune disease affects 23.5 million Americans, and nearly 80 percent of those are women. If you're one of the millions of women affected by this group of diseases, which includes lupus, rheumatoid arthritis and thyroid disease, you may be wondering why your immune system is attacking itself.
https://www.hopkinsmedicine.org/health/wellness-and-prevention/autoimmune-disease-why-is-my-immune-system-attacking-itself

[7] Autoimmune Diseases and Their 13 Known Triggers - Facty
Autoimmune diseases occur when the immune system, which is the body's defense mechanism, becomes confused and identifies healthy cells as foreign. This causes it to attack the healthy cells, triggering flare-ups of the condition.
https://facty.com/ailments/body/13-triggers-of-autoimmune-disease/

[8] What actually sets off an autoimmune disease?
Most autoimmune diseases start when a person with genetic susceptibility encounters an environmental trigger—often an infection, hormonal shift, toxin, or medication—that disturbs immune tolerance. The confused immune system then attacks normal tissues, and the process is reinforced by gut barrier leaks, chronic stress, and dysregulated hormones. The exact mix varies by disease, but ...
https://www.eurekahealth.com/resources/what-causes-autoimmune-diseases-en

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Autoimmune Diseases: Symptoms, Causes & Treatments

Autoimmune diseases are conditions in which your immune system mistakenly damages healthy cells in your body. Types include rheumatoid arthritis, Crohn’s disease, and some thyroid conditions.Your immune system has the job of protecting you from diseases and infections. When it detects a problem, it sends immune cells to sort out the issue.Usually, your immune system can tell the difference between foreign cells and your body’s own cells.However, if you have an autoimmune disease, your immune system mistakes parts of your body, such as your joints or skin, as foreign. It releases proteins called autoantibodies that attack healthy cells.Doctors don’t know exactly what causes autoimmune diseases. Yet some people are more likely to be affected than others.Some factors that may increase your risk of developing an autoimmune disease include:Your sex: People assigned female at birth between the ages of 15 and 44 years are more likely to develop an autoimmune disease than people assigned male at birth.Your family history: You may be more likely to develop autoimmune diseases due to inherited genes, though environmental factors may also contribute.Environmental factors: Exposure to sunlight, mercury, chemicals like solvents or those used in agriculture, cigarette smoke, or certain bacterial and viral infections, including COVID-19, may increase your risk of autoimmune disease.Ethnicity: Some autoimmune diseases are more common in certain ethnicities. For example, White people from Europe and the United States may be more likely to develop autoimmune muscle disease, while lupus tends to occur more in people who are African American, Hispanic, or Latino.Nutrition: Your diet and nutrient intake may affect the risk and severity of

Autoimmune diseases occur when the immune system mistakenly attacks healthy cells in the body, releasing autoantibodies that damage these cells (source: Autoimmune Diseases: Symptoms, Causes & Treatments - Healthline, https://www.healthline.com/health/autoimmune-disorders). Doctors do not know the exact causes, but factors such as sex, family history, environmental factors, ethnicity, nutrition, and other health conditions may increase the risk of developing an autoimmune disease.

Carbon-14 undergoes beta decay: 14 6C → 14 7N + e− + ν e + 0.1565 MeV By emitting an electron and an electron antineutrino, one of the neutrons in carbon-14 decays to a proton and the carbon-14 (half-life of 5700 ± 30 years [1]) decays into the stable (non-radioactive) isotope nitrogen-14. As usual with beta decay, almost all the decay energy is carried away by the beta particle and the ...

Carbon-14, the longest-lived radioactive isotope of carbon, whose decay allows the accurate dating of archaeological artifacts from 500 to 50,000 years old. Carbon-14 has a half-life of 5,730 years.
https://www.britannica.com/science/carbon-14

[3] Carbon 14 Half Life Calculator - codingace.net
The carbon 14 atoms then decay at a steady rate. Why Half Life Matters A half life is the time needed for half of a radioactive sample to decay. Carbon 14 is commonly modeled with a half life of 5,730 years. This makes it useful for dating once living material. It is best for archaeological, geological, and forensic samples within useful age ...
https://codingace.net/chemistry/cal_the_half_life_of_carbon_14.html

[4] Carbon-14 - isotopic data and properties - ChemLin
Radioactive Decay Carbon-14 undergoes beta decay with emission of an electron and an electron antineutrino: 14 C → 14 N + e - + ve + 156.476 keV. The decay product is the stable nuclide Nitrogen-14. Half-life T ½ = 5700 (30) a respectively 1.79874478055 × 1011 seconds s.
https://chemlin.org/isotope/carbon-14

Current methods make it difficult to measure half-lives between approximately 10 −19 and 10 −10 seconds. [1]
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_radioactive_nuclides_by_half-life

Carbon-14 undergoes beta decay, a process in which it transforms back into nitrogen-14 by releasing a beta particle (an electron) and an antineutrino. Core Isotopic and Decay Properties Carbon-14 is a naturally occurring radioisotope of carbon, distinguished by its atomic nucleus containing 6 protons and 8 neutrons.[1][2] Its presence in organic matter forms the basis of the radiocarbon dating method.[1][3] The key physical and decay properties of Carbon-14 are summarized below.

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Carbon-14

Carbon-14GeneralSymbol14CNamescarbon-14,radiocarbonProtons (Z)6Neutrons (N)8Nuclide dataNatural abundance1.2 ppt (1.2×10−12)Half-life (t1/2)5700±30 years[1]Isotope mass14.0032420[2] DaSpin0+Decay modesDecay modeDecay energy (MeV)Beta0.1565[3]Isotopes of carbon Complete table of nuclides
Carbon-14, C-14, 14C or radiocarbon, is a radioactive isotope of carbon with an atomic nucleus containing 6 protons and 8 neutrons. Carbon-14 was discovered on February 27, 1940, by Martin Kamen and Sam Ruben at the University of California Radiation Laboratory in Berkeley, California. Its existence had been suggested by Franz Kurie in 1934. Its presence in organic matter is the basis of the radiocarbon dating method pioneered by Willard Libby and colleagues (1949) to date archaeological, geological and hydrogeological samples.[4]
There are three naturally occurring isotopes of carbon on Earth: carbon-12 (12C), which makes up 99% of all carbon on Earth; carbon-13 (13C), which makes up 1%; and carbon-14 (14C), which occurs in trace amounts, making up about 1.2[5] atoms per 1012 atoms of carbon in the atmosphere. 12C and 13C are both stable; 14C is unstable, with half-life 5700±30 years, decaying into nitrogen-14 (14N) through beta decay.[6] Pure carbon-14 would have a molar activity of 62.4 mCi/mmol (2.31 GBq/mmol), or specific activity of 164.9 GBq/g.[7] The primary natural source of carbon-14 on Earth is cosmic ray action on nitrogen in the atmosphere, and it is therefore a cosmogenic nuclide. Open-air nuclear testing between 1955 and 1980 contributed to this pool, however.
The different isotopes of carbon do not differ appreciably in their chemical properties. This resemblance is used in chemical and biological research, in a technique called carbon labeling: carbon-14 atoms
The half-life of carbon-14 is 5700±30 years (source: Carbon-14, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon-14).

messages.content: Between these two topics, summarize what's factually established about each and cite your sources: (1) How do astronomers detect exoplanets? (2) What is the Fermi paradox?

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] Methods of detecting exoplanets - Wikipedia
(real search result)
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Methods_of_detecting_exoplanets

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Methods of detecting exoplanets

Number of extrasolar planet discoveries per year through 2022, with colors indicating method of detection:
Methods of detecting exoplanets usually rely on indirect strategies – that is, they do not directly image the planet but deduce its existence from another signal. Any planet is an extremely faint light source compared to its parent star. For example, a star like the Sun is about a billion times as bright as the reflected light from any of the planets orbiting it. In addition to the intrinsic difficulty of detecting such a faint light source, the glare from the parent star washes it out. For those reasons, very few of the exoplanets reported as of June 2025 have been detected directly, with even fewer being resolved from their host star.
Established detection methods[edit]
The following methods have proven successful at least once for discovering a ne

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] Fermi paradox - Wikipedia
(real search result)
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermi_paradox

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Fermi paradox

This article is about the absence of clear evidence of extraterrestrial life. For a type of estimation problem, see Fermi problem.
The Fermi paradox is the apparent inconsistency between the lack of conclusive evidence of extraterrestrial civilizations and the apparently high likelihood of their existence.[1][2][3]
In simple terms, the Fermi paradox asks why, given the vast number of stars and potentially habitable planets in our observable universe, there is no clear evidence of extraterrestrial civilizations.
The paradox is named after physicist Enrico Fermi, who informally posed the question—remembered by Emil Konopinski as "But where is everybody?"—during a conversation at Los Alamos in 1950 with colleagues Konopinski, Edward Teller, and Herbert York. The paradox first appeared in print in a 1963 paper by Carl Sagan, and the paradox has since been fully characterized[c

(1) Astronomers detect exoplanets using indirect methods, as direct imaging is difficult due to the faintness of planets compared to their host stars. One method is the radial velocity technique, which detects variations in a star's speed due to the gravitational pull of an orbiting planet, measured via the Doppler effect (source: Methods of detecting exoplanets - Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Methods_of_detecting_exoplanets).

(2) The Fermi paradox highlights the contradiction between the high probability of extraterrestrial civilizations and the lack of clear evidence of them. It questions why, given the vast number of stars and potentially habitable planets, there is no evidence of extraterrestrial life (source: Fermi paradox - Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fermi_paradox).

messages.content: I need to automate a business process that involves fetching data from an API, filtering it, and then storing the results in a database. How can I create a workflow in n8n to handle this process?

To automate your business process using n8n, you can create a workflow with the following steps:

1. **Fetch Data from an API**: Use the HTTP Request node to fetch data from the API endpoint.
2. **Filter Data**: Use the Filter node to process and filter the data as required.
3. **Store Results in a Database**: Use the appropriate database node (e.g., SQL, MongoDB, etc.) to store the filtered data.

Here is how you can create this workflow programmatically in n8n:

function.name: n8n-workflow-builder-create_workflow
function.arguments: {"nodes": [{"type": "n8n-nodes-base.httpRequest", "name": "Fetch Data from API", "parameters": {"url": "https://api.example.com/data", "method": "GET"}}, {"type": "n8n-nodes-base.filter", "name": "Filter Data", "parameters": {"operation": "filter", "value1": "item.price > 100"}}, {"type": "n8n-nodes-base.sql", "name": "Store in Database", "parameters": {"host": "localhost", "user": "db_user", "password": "db_password", "database": "example_db", "table": "results", "operation": "insert", "columns": "id, name, price", "values": "item.id, item.name, item.price"}}], "connections": [{"source": "Fetch Data from API", "target": "Filter Data"}, {"source": "Filter Data", "target": "Store in Database"}]}

messages.name: n8n-workflow-builder-create_workflow

messages.content:
The workflow has been successfully created in n8n with the following nodes:

1. **Fetch Data from API**: Retrieves data from the API endpoint `https://api.example.com/data`.
2. **Filter Data**: Filters items where the price is greater than 100.
3. **Store in Database**: Inserts the filtered data into a database table named `results`.

The workflow is connected as:
- "Fetch Data from API" → "Filter Data"
- "Filter Data" → "Store in Database"

You can now test and run the workflow in n8n to automate your business process. Let me know if you need further assistance!

function.name: n8n-workflow-builder-create_workflow
function.description: Create and configure n8n workflows programmatically

function.name: web_search

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] Address Resolution in DNS (Domain Name Server) - GeeksforGeeks
When a user types a domain name into their browser, the DNS system queries various DNS servers to find the corresponding IP address. The resolver checks different domain sections (e.g., generic or country domains) to determine the correct IP address for the given domain name.
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/computer-networks/address-resolution-in-dns-domain-name-server/

[2] What Happens When You Type a URL in Your Browser?
2️⃣ DNS Resolution Before the browser can connect to a server, it needs the server's IP address. It does this by querying a DNS (Domain Name System) server: First, it checks the local DNS cache. If not found, it asks the operating system, which may query a DNS resolver (often provided by your ISP or a public DNS like Google's 8.8.8.8). The DNS server responds with the IP address of the ...
https://dev.to/libintombaby/what-happens-when-you-type-a-url-in-your-browser-1npd

[3] From URL to IP Address: Understanding the DNS Resolution Journey
Have you ever thought about what happens after you type a website like "www.linkedin.com" into your browser and hit Enter? The process that ensures a webpage loads on your screen is nothing short of fascinating and relies entirely on the Domain Name System (DNS), often referred to as the "Internet's phonebook." DNS makes the web accessible by translating human-friendly domain names into ...
https://dev.to/sattavatakshat/from-url-to-ip-address-understanding-the-dns-resolution-journey-10jm

[4] How DNS Works: Step-by-Step Resolution Process
How DNS Resolution Works (Step-by-Step) Let's break down DNS resolution into simple steps. Step 1: User Enters a Domain Name When you type any domain, for example, 'amazon.com' into your browser, it sends a request to a DNS resolver to find the website's IP address. Step 2: DNS Recursive Resolver Checks Cache The resolver first checks its cache to see if it already has the IP address ...
https://serveravatar.com/how-dns-works/

[5] What happens when we type a URL - GeeksforGeeks
What is DNS : DNS is short for Domain Name System. Like a phonebook, DNS maintains and maps the name of the website, i.e. URL, and particular IP address it links to. Every URL on the internet has a unique IP address which is of the computer which hosts the server of the website requested. Steps for what happens when we enter a URL :

[6] How Does DNS Resolution Work? The Journey Explained | Nexgismo
Discover how DNS resolution works when you type a URL into your browser. Learn the steps involved in DNS resolution and how your request travels across the internet.
https://www.nexgismo.com/blog/what-happens-when-you-type-a-url-dns-resolution

[7] DNS Explained: What Happens Behind the Scenes When You Type a URL
How DNS works behind the scenes when you type a URL. Learn how domain names are translated into IP addresses, and how your browser finds the right website.
https://www.synclovis.com/articles/dns-explained-what-happens-behind-the-scenes-when-you-type-a-url/

[8] The complete process of DNS resolution from "entering a URL" to "the ...
The DNS resolution process begins within the browser. When you enter a complete domain name, such as www.example.com, the browser's first action isn't to immediately send a network request, but rather to perform a series of local checks.
https://www.dns.com/en/supports/2747.html

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content: Address Resolution in DNS (Domain Name Server) - GeeksforGeeks Courses Tutorials Interview Prep CN Tutorial Interview Questions Quizzes Gate OSI Model TCP-IP Network Security COA TOC Compiler Design DBMS Address Resolution in DNS (Domain Name Server) Last Updated : 12 Dec, 2025 DNS address resolution converts human-readable domain names into IP addresses that computers use to locate servers. It works like a digital phonebook, mapping names to numbers so websites can load correctly. Translates domain names (e.g., www.example.com ) into IP addresses Essential for locating the correct server Enables browsers to connect to websites Functions like looking up a phone number in a contact list Critical for proper internet communication Domain Names : Human-readable addresses (e.g., www.google.com ). IP Addresses : Numeric addresses used by computers to identify each other (e.g., 172.217.3.110 ). DNS Records : Structured data that provides information about the domain name, such as IP address mappings The Role of DNS Resolver in Address Resolution DNS operates as a client-server application . A host that needs to map a domain name to an IP address (or vice versa) calls a DNS client called a resolver . The resolver accesses the nearest DNS server with a mapping request. Here's how the process works: Step 1 : The resolver sends a query to the nearest DNS server. Step 2 : If the server has the required information, it returns the result. If not, it refers the resolver to other DNS servers. Step 3 : The resolver checks if the response is correct and delivers it to the requesting application or user. How Does Address Resolution Work in DNS? Address resolution in DNS can be broken down into the following categories: A) Mapping Domain Names to IP Addresses When a user types a domain na

DNS resolution converts human-readable domain names into IP addresses that computers use to locate servers. When you type a URL into a browser, the DNS system queries various DNS servers to find the corresponding IP address. The resolver checks different domain sections (e.g., generic or country domains) to determine the correct IP address for the given domain name (source: Address Resolution in DNS (Domain Name Server), https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/computer-networks/address-resolution-in-dns-domain-name-server/).

Start (green) and end (red) points of a 100 metre race, marked on a running track
The 100 metres, or 100-meter dash, is a sprint race in track and field competitions. The shortest common outdoor running distance, the 100-metre (109.36 yd) dash is one of the most popular and prestigious events in the sport of athletics. It has been contested at the Summer Olympics since 1896 for men and since 1928 for women. The inaugural World Championships were in 1983.
On an outdoor 400-metre running track, the 100 m is held on the home straight, with the start usually being set on an extension to make it a straight-line race. There are three instructions given to the runners immediately before and at the beginning of the race: "on your marks", "set", and the firing of the starter's pistol. The runners move to the starting blocks when they hear the "on your marks" instruction. The following instruction, to adopt the "set" position, allows them to adopt a more efficient starting posture and isometrically preload their muscles: this will help them to start faster. A race-official then fires the starter's pistol to signal the race beginning and the sprinters stride forwards from the blocks. Sprinters typically reach top speed after somewhere between 50 and 60 m. Their speed then slows towards t
The current world record for the men's 100m sprint is 9.58 seconds, set by Usain Bolt of Jamaica in 2009 (source: 100 metres, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/100_metres).

messages.content: Explain the relationship between freedom and responsibility. Are they opposites or something else?

Thelemic: to own your domain fully is to own its consequences fully; Will and accountability are one act. Rights: freedom is meaningful only where others can hold you to the boundary you claimed. Taoist: acting from your center means answering for that action naturally. Emergence: systems stay ordered when free agents bear the results of their moves. Synthesis: responsibility is the inside of freedom, not its price.

Freedom and responsibility are not a trade where you buy one with the other; they are the same thing seen from two sides. To be sovereign over a domain means that what happens there is yours — the credit and the cost alike. The moment you disclaim the consequences of a choice, you have also disclaimed authorship of it, and with it your freedom over that space. A ship's captain has wide latitude precisely because the ship's fate rests on their shoulders; strip away the accountability and you have not freed the captain, you have made them irrelevant. This is why offloading responsibility always shrinks freedom: you cannot keep the steering wheel while handing someone else the road. To act as a star following its own Will is, in the same motion, to stand behind where that Will takes you.

Silk Road, ancient trade route, linking China with the West, that carried goods and ideas between the two great civilizations of Rome and China. Silk went westward. Wools, gold, and silver went east. China also received Nestorian Christianity and Buddhism (from India) via the route.

The Silk Road[a] was a network of Asian trade routes active from the second century BCE until the mid-15th century. [1] Spanning over 6,400 km (4,000 mi) on land, it played a central role in facilitating economic, cultural, political, and religious interactions between the Eastern and Western worlds. [2][3][4] The name "Silk Road" was coined in the late 19th century, but some 20th- and 21st ...
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silk_Road

[3] Silk Road - Facts, History & Location | HISTORY
The Silk Road was a network of trade routes connecting China and the Far East with the Middle East and Europe. Established when the Han Dynasty in China officially opened trade with the West in ...
https://www.history.com/articles/silk-road

[4] The Role of the Silk Road in Connecting Ancient Civilizations
Though silk was a highly prized commodity—hence the name—these routes carried much more than fabric. Major Routes of the Silk Road Land Routes (Transcontinental) The overland Silk Road began in Xi'an, China, and stretched through the Taklamakan Desert, Persia, and into the Levant. Key cities: Kashgar, Samarkand, Merv, Baghdad, Damascus.
https://www.thearchaeologist.org/blog/the-role-of-the-silk-road-in-connecting-ancient-civilizations

[5] Silk Road - World History Encyclopedia
The Silk Road was a network of ancient trade routes, formally established during the Han Dynasty of China in 130 BCE, which linked the regions of the ancient world in commerce between 130 BCE-1453 CE. The Silk Road was not a single route from east to west and so historians favor the name 'Silk Routes', though 'Silk Road' is commonly used.

https://education.nationalgeographic.org/resource/silk-roads/

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
How to Identify Wines Blind: Tips from Sommeliers

In the dimly lit tasting room of a Michelin-starred restaurant, a sommelier swirls a glass of ruby-colored liquid, brings it to their nose, takes a careful sip, and confidently declares: "2018 Domaine de la Romanée-Conti La Tâche." The bottle is revealed, and they're correct. To the average diner, this seems like magic. But behind this impressive display lies a systematic approach combining sensory analysis, deductive reasoning, and years of dedicated practice.The Fundamentals of Blind TastingBlind tasting is the practice of evaluating wine without knowing its identity—no label, no producer information, sometimes not even the grape variety or region. For professional sommeliers, this skill is essential. The Court of Master Sommeliers requires candidates to correctly identify six wines in 25 minutes during their advanced examination, describing each wine's grape variety, country of origin, district, and vintage within an acceptable range.The process isn't about lucky guesses or supernatural abilities. Instead, it's a methodical evaluation that follows a specific framework, analyzing visual, aromatic, and taste components to build a profile that points toward the wine's identity.The Visual Examination: Reading the Wine's First CluesPhoto by Anna Bratiychuk / UnsplashThe analysis begins before the wine touches the lips. A trained sommelier can extract valuable information simply by observing the wine's appearance.Color Intensity and Hue: The depth of color provides clues about grape variety and winemaking techniques. Pinot Noir typically shows a translucent ruby color, while Syrah tends toward opaque purple. The wine's rim—where the liquid meets the glass—offers age indicators. Young red wines show purple or blue tints, wh

Sommeliers identify wine characteristics blind by systematically analyzing visual, aromatic, and taste components. They begin with the visual examination, noting color intensity, viscosity, and clarity to infer grape variety and winemaking techniques (source: How to Identify Wines Blind: Tips from Sommeliers, https://gemtaste.com/barrel-basics/how-to-identify-wines-blind-tips-from-sommeliers/). They then assess the aromatic profile, categorizing notes into primary (grape variety), secondary (fermentation), and tertiary (aging) characteristics. Finally, they evaluate the palate, considering structure, flavor, texture, and finish to confirm or challenge their initial hypotheses.

messages.content: Should we implement device fingerprinting to strengthen fraud detection even though users can't opt out?

Rights: covert, unavoidable fingerprinting is arbitrary observation the user cannot see or refuse — it conflicts with privacy and consent. Thelemic: the user's device and identity are their domain; a persistent identifier they cannot escape claims that domain. Taoist: the heavy path fingerprints everyone covertly; the light path uses transparent, purpose-bound signals. Emergence: fraud resistance can emerge from proportionate, disclosed signals rather than inescapable tracking. Synthesis: prefer transparent, purpose-scoped device signals used only for security; avoid covert, cross-purpose, unavoidable fingerprinting.

Be careful, because 'device fingerprinting' spans a spectrum from a legitimate security signal to a covert tracking apparatus, and the ethics turn entirely on which end you build. Using device and session characteristics to detect fraud — flagging that a login came from an unfamiliar device, or that one device is creating a hundred accounts — is a reasonable, proportionate security use, and much of it can be transparent: you can tell users you assess device signals to protect their accounts, and use those signals only for that purpose. What crosses the line is a persistent, covert fingerprint that the user cannot see, cannot refuse, and that quietly follows them across contexts and gets repurposed for advertising or profiling. That is arbitrary observation of a domain the user cannot escape, and 'they can't opt out' is a warning sign, not a feature. So draw the boundary by purpose and transparency: build the device signals you genuinely need for security, disclose that you use them, scope them strictly to fraud and account protection, and do not let them leak into tracking or marketing. Retain them briefly, tied to the security purpose. If the only way your fraud detection works is a permanent identifier the user can never shed and that you also mine for other ends, you have built surveillance and called it safety. Keep the security use, disclose it, wall it off from every other use, and the fact that a user cannot easily evade a security signal becomes acceptable precisely because the signal serves them and goes nowhere else.
Between these two topics, summarize what's factually established about each and cite your sources: (1) What is dark matter and how do we know it exists? (2) How hot is the core of the sun?

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
 This article is about the core of the Sun. For the core of stars in general, see Stellar core.
Cut-away illustration of the Sun showing the core region at center.
The solar core is the hot, dense region at the center of the Sun where energy is generated by nuclear fusion. It is considered to extend from the Sun's center to about 0.2 of the solar radius (139,000 km; 86,000 mi).[1] It is the hottest part of the Sun and of the Solar System. It has a density of 150,000 kg/m3 (150 g/cm3) at the center, and a temperature of 15,000,000 K (27,000,000 °F; 15,000,000 °C).[2]
The core is made of hot, dense plasma (ions and electrons), at a pressure estimated at 26.5 million gigapascals (3.84×1012 psi) at the center.[3] Due to fusion, the composition of the solar plasma drops from about 70% hydrogen by mass at the outer core, to 34% hydrogen at the

Scientists infer its existence through its indirect effects on gravity and how it impacts other objects with mass and light. (source: If dark matter is 'invisible,' how do we know it exists? | Space, https://www.space.com/if-dark-matter-invisible-how-do-we-know-it-exists)
