messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Tides and Water Levels, What Causes Tides, NOS Education Offering

What Causes Tides?
Gravity is one major force that creates tides. In 1687, Sir Isaac Newton explained that ocean tides result from the gravitational attraction of the sun and moon on the oceans of the earth (Sumich, J.L., 1996).
Newton’s law of universal gravitation states that the gravitational attraction between two bodies is directly proportional to their masses, and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the bodies (Sumich, J.L., 1996; Thurman, H.V., 1994). Therefore, the greater the mass of the objects and the closer they are to each other, the greater the gravitational attraction between them (Ross, D.A. 1995).
Tidal forces are based on the gravitational attractive force. With regard to tidal forces on the Earth, the distance between two objects usually is more critical than their masses. Tidal generating forces vary inversely as the cube of the distance from the tide generating object. Gravitational attractive forces only vary inversely to the square of the distance between the objects (Thurman, H.V., 1994). The effect of distance on tidal forces is seen in the relationship between the sun, the moon, and the Earth’s waters.
Our sun is 27 million times larger than our moon. Based on its mass, the sun's gravitational attraction to the Earth is more than 177 times greater than that of the moon to the Earth. If tidal forces were based solely on comparative masses, the sun should have a tide-generating force that is 27 million times greater than that of the moon. However, the sun is 390 times further from the Earth than is the moon. Thus, its tide-generating force is reduced by 3903, or about 59 million times less than the moon. Because of these conditions, the sun’s tide-gene

Tides on Earth are primarily caused by the gravitational attraction of the moon and the sun (source: What Causes Tides - Tides and Water Levels: NOAA's National Ocean ..., https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/education/tutorial_tides/tides02_cause.html). The moon, despite being smaller than the sun, has a greater tidal influence because it is much closer to Earth, making its gravitational pull more effective in creating tides (source: What Causes Tides - Tides and Water Levels: NOAA's National Ocean ..., https://oceanservice.noaa.gov/education/tutorial_tides/tides02_cause.html).

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content: CAP Theorem in System Design - GeeksforGeeks Courses Tutorials Interview Prep System Design Tutorial HLD LLD Functional and Non Functional Life Cycle Design Patterns UML Diagrams System Design Interview Guide Scalability Databases DSA Software Engineering CAP Theorem in System Design Last Updated : 30 Apr, 2026 According to the CAP theorem, only two of the three desirable characteristics consistency, availability, and partition tolerance can be shared or present in a networked shared-data system or distributed system. The theorem provides a way of thinking about the trade-offs involved in designing and building distributed systems. It helps to explain why certain types of systems may be more appropriate for certain use cases. The theorem states that a distributed system can have at most two of these guarantees. CAP Theorem Properties The property of three distributed system characteristics to which CAP Theorem refers: Properties of CAP Theorem 1. Consistency Consistency defines that all clients see the same data simultaneously, no matter which node they connect to in a distributed system. For eventual consistency, the guarantees are a bit loose. Eventual consistency guarantee means client will eventually see the same data on all the nodes at some point of time in the future. Consistency In the above Diagram All nodes in the system see the same data at the same time. This is because the nodes are constantly communicating with each other and sharing updates.  Any changes made to the data on one node are immediately propagated to all other nodes, ensuring that everyone has the same up-to-date information. 2. Availability Availability defines that all non-failing nodes in a distributed system return a response for all read and write requests in a bounded amount of time, eve

The CAP theorem in distributed systems states that a system can only guarantee two out of the three properties: consistency, availability, and partition tolerance (source: CAP Theorem in System Design - GeeksforGeeks, https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/system-design/cap-theorem-in-system-design/). Consistency ensures all clients see the same data simultaneously, availability means all non-failing nodes return a response for all read and write requests, and partition tolerance allows the system to continue operating despite arbitrary message loss or failure in parts of the system.

messages.content: How can I ensure the reliability of my answer when I need to generate text on a complex topic that might be interpreted differently by various language models?

function.name: multi-llm-cross-check-server-cross_check
function.arguments: {"prompt": "How can I ensure the reliability of my answer when I need to generate text on a complex topic that might be interpreted differently by various language models?"}

messages.content:
It seems there was an issue retrieving responses from the language models. Let me provide some general guidance on how to ensure the reliability of your answer when generating text on complex topics:

1. **Cross-Verification**: Use multiple sources or tools to verify the information. Compare the results from different language models or databases to identify commonalities and discrepancies.

2. **Consult Trusted Sources**: Refer to authoritative or peer-reviewed sources when dealing with complex or sensitive topics. This helps in grounding your response in established knowledge.

3. **Clarify Intent and Context**: Make sure your prompt is clear and well-structured. Adding context can help language models interpret the topic more accurately and consistently.

4. **Use Critical Thinking**: Analyze the output for logical consistency, coherence, and alignment with known facts or principles.

5. **Iterative Refinement**: Refine your prompts based on the responses you receive. Iterating through the process can help improve the accuracy and reliability of the output.

6. **Human Oversight**: Always involve a human reviewer to validate the results, especially when the topic is complex or high-stakes.

Would you like me to suggest some specific strategies or tools for your use case?

function.name: multi-llm-cross-check-server-cross_check
function.description:
Cross-check answers from multiple public LLM APIs given a prompt. To goal is to show a list of answers from different LLMs.

    Arguments:
        prompt (str): The input prompt to send to the LLMs.

    Returns:
        dict: A dictionary containing the responses from each LLM. Each key in the dictionary corresponds to the name of an LLM, and its value is either:
              - The response from the LLM in JSON format (e.g., containing generated text or completions).
              - An error message if the request to the LLM failed.

Dark matter is a mysterious form of matter that makes up around 85% of the total matter in the universe. Scientists know it exists because of its indirect effects on gravity and how it impacts other objects with mass and light. The first hints of dark matter were observed in 1933 when astronomer Fritz Zwicky used the Mount Wilson Observatory to measure the visible mass of the Coma Cluster of galaxies.

messages.content: Our event-driven system has events that are too fine-grained, consumers must process dozens to reconstruct one business fact. Contract design issue?

Taoist: events fragmented below the natural unit of meaning force consumers to do reassembly work that fights the grain.
Thelemic: consumers are sovereign over their logic, but reconstructing a business fact from scattered events imposes your internals on them.
Rights: an event should legibly represent a meaningful fact; too-fine events leak implementation steps consumers shouldn't have to reason about.
Emergence: events pitched at the right granularity, business facts, let consumers react simply and independently.
Synthesis: yes, events should represent meaningful business facts at the right granularity; too-fine-grained events leak internals, so raise the granularity to the domain event level.

Yes, this is a granularity problem in your event contract, and it stems from publishing events at the level of your internal steps rather than at the level of meaningful business facts. When a consumer has to subscribe to dozens of tiny events and stitch them together to learn that, say, "an order was placed," you have effectively leaked your internal processing sequence into the contract: consumers now depend on the order, completeness, and timing of your implementation steps, which is both fragile (a refactor of your internals breaks them) and burdensome (every consumer reimplements the same reassembly logic, and gets it subtly wrong). An event is supposed to be a legible statement of something that happened in the domain, and a fact fragmented across dozens of low-level events is not legible; it is a puzzle.

Raise the granularity to the domain event level: publish the business fact itself, OrderPlaced with the meaningful payload, as a single event that consumers can react to directly, instead of (or alongside) the fine-grained mechanical events. The right altitude is "a thing the business cares about happened," carrying enough data for typical consumers to act without immediately calling back for more. This lets each sovereign consumer react simply and independently to facts that matter to it, and it hides your internal steps behind the boundary so you can change how you produce the fact without breaking anyone. There is a balance, events that are too coarse (a giant "something changed" blob) are also bad, because they force consumers to diff and figure out what actually happened, so aim for the natural unit of meaning in the domain. If some consumers genuinely need the fine-grained detail, you can still emit it on a separate, clearly-purposed stream, but the primary contract should speak in business facts. Reframe the events around what happened in the domain rather than how your system processed it, and the reconstruction burden, and the coupling that comes with it, disappears.

Annual average monsoon precipitation in India over 110 years. Rains that exceed 10% typically lead to major floods, while a 10% shortfall is a significant drought.[2]
The Monsoon of South Asia is among several geographically distributed global monsoons. Mariana Trench

Mariana TrenchMariana TrenchCoordinates11°21′N 142°12′E﻿ / ﻿11.350°N 142.200°ETypeOceanic trenchOcean/sea sourcesPacific OceanMax. length2,550 km (1,580 mi)Max. width69 km (43 mi)Max. depth10,935 ± 6 metres (35,876 ± 20 ft; 5,979.3 ± 3.3 fathoms; 6.7947 ± 0.0037 mi) (Challenger Deep)LocationInteractive map of Mariana Trench
Location of the Mariana Trench
The Mariana Trench is the deepest oceanic trench on Earth, located in the western Pacific Ocean, about 200 kilometres (124 mi) east of the Mariana Islands. It is crescent-shaped and measures about 2,550 km (1,580 mi) in length and 69 km (43 mi) in width. The maximum known depth is 10,935 ± 6 metres (35,876 ± 20 ft; 5,979.3 ± 3.3 fathoms; 6.7947 ± 0.0037 mi) at the southern end of a small slot-shaped valley in its floor known as the Challenger Deep.[1] This makes the deepest point of the trench more than 2 km (1.2 mi) fart

(1) The monsoon season in South Asia is influenced by the unique geographical features of the Indian subcontinent, along with atmospheric, oceanic, and geographical factors. It is a seasonal wind system that brings significant rainfall from June through September, affecting agriculture and the economies of several countries in the region (source: Monsoon of South Asia - Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monsoon_of_South_Asia).

(2) The Mariana Trench is the deepest oceanic trench on Earth, with its maximum known depth at the Challenger Deep being 10,935 ± 6 metres (35,876 ± 20 feet) below sea level. It is located in the western Pacific Ocean, about 200 kilometres (124 miles) east of the Mariana Islands (source: Mariana Trench - Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mariana_Trench).

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orbital_speed

Rockets achieve this through gravity turns—gradually pitching over during ascent to build up horizontal velocity while still climbing. By the time the rocket reaches orbital altitude, most of its velocity is horizontal, with just enough vertical component to maintain altitude. The precise timing and angle of the gravity turn is critical.

[7] Achieving Orbit: Understanding Rocket Launches Dynamics

https://spacevoyageventures.com/achieving-orbit-the-mechanics-behind-rocket-launches/

[8] Ideal Rocket Equation - Glenn Research Center | NASA
Ideal Rocket Equation On this page: The forces on a rocket change dramatically during a typical flight. During powered flight, the propellants of the propulsion system are constantly being exhausted from the nozzle. As a result, the weight and mass of the rocket is constantly changing. Because of the changing mass, we cannot use the standard form of Newton's second law of motion to determine ...

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Flight To Orbit | Glenn Research Center | NASA

Study Of Rockets
The study of rockets is an excellent way for students to learn the basics of forces and the response of an object to external forces. All rockets use the thrust generated by a propulsion system to overcome the weight of the rocket. The aerodynamic drag and lift are important forces acting on the toy rockets, like stomp rockets, bottle rockets, and model rockets. For air-to-air and ground-to-air missiles, the aerodynamic forces are also significant. Aerodynamic forces are not as important for satellite launchers, because of the flight trajectory to orbit. The rocket gets out of the atmosphere as quickly as possible, then gains the velocity needed to remain in orbit.
Thrust and Weight
On this page we show the major events in the flight of a two-stage launch to orbit. Throughout the flight, the weight of the rocket is constantly changing because of the burning of the propellants. At launch, the thrust produced by the engine is greater than the weight of the rocket and the net force accelerates the rocket away from the pad. Unlike model rockets, full scale launchers rely on a sophisticated guidance system to balance and steer the rocket during its flight. The thrust of the rocket is gimbaled, or rotated, during the flight to produce maneuvers. Leaving the pad, the rocket begins a powered vertical ascent. The vehicle accelerates because of the high thrust and decreasing weight and rather quickly moves out of the thick atmosphere near the surface of the earth. Although the rocket is traveling supersonically, the drag on the vehicle is small because of the shape of the rocket and the lower air density at altitude. As the rocket ascends, it also begins to pitch over, and its flight path becomes more inclined to th

Rockets achieve orbital velocity by first escaping the Earth's atmosphere and then gaining the necessary speed to maintain a stable orbit. This involves a combination of altitude and horizontal velocity, where the rocket must reach a specific velocity depending on its altitude above the Earth's surface. The exact speed needed to orbit the Earth depends on the altitude, as described by a formula developed by Johannes Kepler in the early 1600s (source: Flight To Orbit - Glenn Research Center | NASA, https://www1.grc.nasa.gov/beginners-guide-to-aeronautics/flight-to-orbit/).

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
How Your Immune System Remembers What You've Fought

Infectious disease control, vaccine durability, and immune-based therapies all rely on one key principle: immune memory. 
Understanding how the immune system remembers past encounters with pathogens—and how it responds to reinfections—is essential for developing better public health strategies.
Image Credit: Motortion Films/Shutterstock.com
What Triggers Long-Term Immune Memory
Immune memory is a defining feature of the adaptive immune system. It forms the basis of vaccination and explains why people who recover from an infection are often better protected if exposed again to the same pathogen.
Unlike innate immune responses, which act quickly and uniformly, adaptive responses are highly specific. They also provide long-lasting protection. This protection is driven by memory B cells and T cells. These cells retain information about past infections and enable a faster, stronger response when the same pathogen returns.1,2
Studying the cellular and molecular basis of immune memory helps us understand how vaccines work. It also guides the development of better strategies to protect against persistent and emerging infections.
Download your PDF copy now!
Cells Involved in Immune Memory
Memory B Cells
Memory B cells are key components of humoral immunity. They develop from germinal center reactions after exposure to an antigen. These cells undergo somatic hypermutation and isotype switching, which produces high-affinity, antigen-specific clones. When exposed again to the same antigen, memory B cells quickly differentiate into antibody-secreting plasma cells. This allows for rapid neutralization of the pathogen.2
Some subsets, such as immunoglobulin (Ig)M⁺ memory B cells and B1 cells, have memory-like functions without needin

The human immune system remembers past infections through memory B cells and T cells, which retain information about pathogens and enable a faster, stronger response upon re-exposure (source: How Your Immune System Remembers What You've Fought, https://www.azolifesciences.com/article/How-Your-Immune-System-Remembers-What-Youve-Fought.aspx). Memory B cells differentiate into antibody-secreting plasma cells, while memory T cells provide long-term cellular immunity by mounting rapid effector functions (source: How Your Immune System Remembers What You've Fought, https://www.azolifesciences.com/article/How-Your-Immune-System-Remembers-What-Youve-Fought.aspx).

messages.content: My team wants to rewrite our frontend in the newest framework because the current one is 'outdated.' I'm skeptical but don't want to seem like I'm blocking progress. How do I evaluate this honestly?

Emergence: a rewrite is a large, hard-to-reverse bet that should be justified by evidence of concrete problems and value, not by 'outdated' as an aesthetic. Rights: the team and org deserve an honest case, separating real pain from novelty-seeking. Taoist: resist the pull to rewrite for its own sake; the grain favors keeping what works and changing only for real gain. Thelemic: the team's enthusiasm is legitimate energy, but it owes the org a grounded justification, not a fashion argument. Synthesis: reframe from 'outdated vs progress' to 'what concrete problem does this solve and is a rewrite the cheapest fix', demand evidence and a reversible path, and honor the team's energy by aiming it at the real problem.

You're not blocking progress by asking for the case, you're insisting progress be real rather than fashionable, and that's your job. 'Outdated' is an aesthetic complaint, not a business reason, so redirect the conversation from the framework's age to the concrete problems it's causing: is it actually slowing feature delivery, is it unmaintainable, is it a hiring liability, is it hitting real performance or capability limits? Ask the team to name the specific pains and quantify them, because a rewrite is one of the largest, hardest-to-reverse bets a team can make, and it must be justified by problems worth that cost, not by the appeal of the new. Then test whether a rewrite is even the cheapest fix for those problems; often the real pain can be addressed incrementally, upgrading, adopting the new framework module by module behind a boundary, or fixing the specific bad patterns, all of which stay reversible in a way a big-bang rewrite never is. Honor the team's energy rather than crushing it: their enthusiasm is real and useful, so point it at the actual problem and let them prove the case with a bounded pilot that migrates one real slice and measures whether it delivers the claimed benefits. If the evidence is strong, you have a grounded path forward and you were never a blocker; if it evaporates under scrutiny, you've saved the team from months of rewriting their way back to roughly where they started, just newer.

messages.content:
[1] 8 Reasons Why Rome Fell: The Fall of Rome Explained | HISTORY
Historians cite multiple overlapping factors for the fall of the Western Roman Empire, ranging from economic collapse to military defeat. Here are the major reasons Rome fell.
https://www.history.com/articles/8-reasons-why-rome-fell

[2] Fall of the Western Roman Empire - Wikipedia
The fall of the Western Roman Empire, also called the fall of the Roman Empire or the fall of Rome, was the loss of central political control in the Western Roman Empire, a process in which the Empire failed to enforce its rule, and its vast territory was divided among several successor polities. The Roman Empire lost the strengths that had allowed it to exercise effective control over its ...
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire

[3] Fall of the Roman Empire: Year, Internal & External Causes
The fall of the Roman Empire in AD 476 marked a pivotal moment in history, leading to the end of an era that had seen the empire stretch from Britain to North Africa and from Spain to the Middle East. While the exact reasons for the fall are complex and multifaceted, historians have identified several key internal and external factors that contributed to this dramatic collapse.
https://romanempirehistory.com/fall-of-the-roman-empire/

[4] What Factors Caused the Fall of the Roman Empire?
The Roman Empire fell slowly over hundreds of years, not in a single sudden event. Many factors like invasions, internal decay, and economic troubles contributed to the fall of Rome. Different historians suggest various dates and reasons for the fall of Rome, highlighting its complexity.
https://www.thoughtco.com/what-was-the-fall-of-rome-112688

[5] Why Did the Roman Empire Fall? Corruption, Inflation, and Division
It's one of the most debated questions in world history: Why did the Roman Empire fall? The answer isn't simple, because Rome didn't fall in a single moment. Instead, the decline of the Western Roman Empire was a long, complex process shaped by many internal and external pressures.
https://history.howstuffworks.com/european-history/why-did-the-roman-empire-fall.htm

[6] The Fall of Rome: When, Why, and How Did Rome Fall?
Learn about the complex process of the fall of Rome and the Western Roman Empire, which took place over several centuries and culminated in 476 AD. Explore the economic, political, military, and social factors that contributed to Rome's decline, as well as the external threats from barbarian invasions.
https://historycooperative.org/the-fall-of-rome-last-days-of-empire/

[7] Why Did Rome Fall? Inside The Empire's Collapse
In short, the fall of Rome was not due to a single cause but a myriad of things — many of which are still debated by historians to this day. From A Ramshackle Village To The Roman Empire The fall of Rome came like a ticking clock, century by century.
https://allthatsinteresting.com/why-did-rome-fall

[8] The Fall Of The Roman Empire - 8 Critical Causes - History Hogs
Learn how overexpansion, division, slave decline, legion weakening, imperial collapse, barbarian invasion, and climate change contributed to the downfall of the Western Roman Empire in 476 AD. Explore the complex factors that shaped one of history's most pivotal moments.
https://historyhogs.com/what-caused-the-fall-of-the-roman-empire/

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Roth IRA vs. traditional IRA: What’s the difference? | Fidelity

These accounts offer different tax benefits.
Key takeaways
Both traditional individual retirement accounts (IRAs) and Roth IRAs are retirement savings accounts designed to help you save for retirement.
Traditional IRAs' and Roth IRAs' primary difference is the type of tax advantage you can receive for contributing to and investing in the account.
Although almost anyone can contribute to a traditional IRA, only those whose income is below the IRS's annual limit are eligible to contribute to a Roth IRA.
Individual retirement accounts (IRAs) are a powerful way to help save for retirement—and people across the US are catching on. A 2024 study from the Investment Company Institute found that 44% of US households have an IRA; in 2014, that number was only 34%.1 But before opening an IRA, you might wonder what the best type of account is for your situation. Here's what you need to know about a Roth IRA versus a traditional IRA.
What's a Roth IRA?
A Roth IRA is a tax-advantaged retirement savings account independent from workplace savings plans. You contribute money you've already paid taxes on, which can then be invested and potentially grow federally-tax-free. You can withdraw your contributions at any time, and you can withdraw any investment earnings federally-tax-free, provided you meet certain requirements.2 To contribute to a Roth IRA, though, you have to fall within the IRS's Roth IRA income limits, and you can only contribute up to the annual IRA contribution limit.
Advantages of Roth IRAs
Here are 3 reasons why saving with a Roth IRA might be a smart move:
You can take out what you put in: You're free to withdraw the money you've contributed to your Roth IRA at any time without any taxes or penalties.
Ta

The primary difference between a Roth IRA and a traditional IRA is the type of tax advantage they offer. A Roth IRA allows you to contribute money you've already paid taxes on, and your investments can grow and be withdrawn tax-free, provided certain requirements are met (source: Roth IRA vs. traditional IRA: What’s the difference?, https://www.fidelity.com/learning-center/smart-money/roth-ira-vs-traditional-ira). In contrast, a traditional IRA allows you to deduct contributions from your federal taxes, providing an immediate tax break, but withdrawals are taxed as income in retirement (source: Roth IRA vs. traditional IRA: What’s the difference?, https://www.fidelity.com/learning-center/smart-money/roth-ira-vs-traditional-ira).

function.name: context7-resolve-library-id
function.description:
Resolves a package/product name to a Context7-compatible library ID and returns a list of matching libraries.

You MUST call this function before 'get-library-docs' to obtain a valid Context7-compatible library ID UNLESS the user explicitly provides a library ID in the format '/org/project' or '/org/project/version' in their query.

Selection Process:
1. Analyze the query to understand what library/package the user is looking for
2. Return the most relevant match based on:
- Name similarity to the query (exact matches prioritized)
- Description relevance to the query's intent
- Documentation coverage (prioritize libraries with higher Code Snippet counts)
- Trust score (consider libraries with scores of 7-10 more authoritative)

Response Format:
- Return the selected library ID in a clearly marked section
- Provide a brief explanation for why this library was chosen
- If multiple good matches exist, acknowledge this but proceed with the most relevant one
- If no good matches exist, clearly state this and suggest query refinements

For ambiguous queries, request clarification before proceeding with a best-guess match.

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Hubble vs. Webb - NASA Science

Observatory: Hubble vs. Webb
On The Shoulders of a Giant
Hubble and Webb work together to explore the cosmos. Their observations complement each other, providing us with a broad view of the universe.
Overview
In 1675, famed physicist and mathematician, Isaac Newton, penned a letter to his contemporary and rival, polymath Robert Hooke. In that letter, Newton wrote what would become one of his most famous remarks: “If I have seen further, it is by standing on the shoulders of giants.” Over time, this simple statement has come to represent the process of science itself, each new discovery building upon previous work. The same is true for NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope and its observing partner, NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope.
Since its 1990 launch, the Hubble Space Telescope has given humanity a unique perspective of the universe. From its vantage point above Earth’s light-distorting atmosphere, Hubble offers us clear, detailed views that reach farther out in space and further back in time than any of its predecessors. Hubble’s observations have charted the evolution of galaxies, stars, nebulae, comets, the outer planets, and their moons. Hubble’s discoveries have confirmed the existence of black holes in galaxy cores, measured the composition of exoplanet atmospheres, found the most distant galaxies to date, and verified the accelerating expansion of the universe – a 2011 Nobel Prize winning discovery in physics. Hubble’s findings have affected all aspects of astronomy, prompting further research and revisions to textbooks, but its observations have captured more than our scientific curiosity. Today we find Hubble’s iconic images on lunchboxes, coffee mugs, socks, neckties, and even moving vans. For more than 30 years, Hubble has spurred

The James Webb Space Telescope differs from Hubble in several key ways. Webb is the largest and most technically advanced telescope ever built, with a 21.3-foot (6.5-meter) primary mirror, significantly larger than Hubble's 7.9-foot (2.4-meter) mirror. This gives Webb more than six times the light collecting area, allowing it to detect objects up to 100 times fainter. Webb also has a deeper infrared vision, ranging from 0.6 microns to 28.5 microns, enabling it to peer back over 13.5 billion years to witness the first stars and galaxies forming. In contrast, Hubble is optimized for shorter ultraviolet and visible wavelengths (0.1 microns to 2.5 microns) and has been instrumental in confirming the existence of dark energy and measuring the accelerating expansion of the universe (source: Hubble vs. Webb - NASA Science, https://science.nasa.gov/mission/hubble/observatory/hubble-vs-webb/).

messages.content: A teammate insists we should never use shared mutable state at all — everything immutable, message-passing only. Is that dogma or wisdom for a high-concurrency service?

Taoist: an absolute ban is force; the grain of the problem sometimes genuinely calls for shared state. Wisdom fits the tool to the case.
Thelemic: each piece of state has a nature; some truly want single ownership, some are naturally shared and read-heavy. Respect each.
Rights: engineers deserve non-arbitrary, principled guidance, not an absolute rule to obey.
Emergence: correct concurrent systems emerge from minimizing shared mutability, not from banning it absolutely.
Synthesis: the instinct is right, the absolutism is wrong — prefer immutability and message-passing as defaults, allow guarded shared state where it genuinely fits.

The instinct is wisdom; the absolutism is dogma. Shared mutable state is the single richest source of concurrency bugs — races, deadlocks, torn reads, heisenbugs that vanish under a debugger — so preferring immutability and message-passing as your defaults is exactly right, and it will eliminate whole categories of failure before they can occur. Immutable data can be shared freely because it cannot be corrupted; single-owner actors that communicate by messages localize all mutation, so reasoning stays tractable. For most of a high-concurrency service, these defaults are the correct and safer path.

But "never, at all" ignores that some problems genuinely have shared, mutable, contended state at their core — a high-throughput cache, a connection pool, a counter, a shared queue — where copying or message-passing would impose real cost or complexity for no benefit. There, the right answer is not to pretend the state is not shared but to guard it well: the smallest correct lock, an atomic or lock-free structure, or a purpose-built concurrent collection, confined behind a narrow, well-tested interface so the danger is contained and legible. So make immutability and message-passing the grain you work with by default, minimize shared mutable state relentlessly, and where it genuinely belongs, treat it as a small, carefully-guarded exception rather than a forbidden sin. Principle over prohibition.

Late Bronze Age collapse

Invasions, destruction and population movements during the Late Bronze Age collapse
The Late Bronze Age collapse was a period of societal collapse in the Mediterranean basin during the late 13th to early 12th century BC. It is thought to have affected much of the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East, in particular Egypt, Anatolia, the Aegean, eastern Libya, and the Balkans. The collapse was sudden, violent, and culturally disruptive for many Bronze Age civilizations, creating a sharp material decline for the region's previously existing powers.
The palace economy of Mycenaean Greece, the Aegean region, and Anatolia that characterized the Late Bronze Age disintegrated, transforming into the small isolated village cultures of the Greek Dark Ages, which lasted from c. 1100 to c. 750 BC, and were followed by the better-known Archaic Age. messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
How the Black Death Ended Feudalism and Freed the Serfs

The Black Death, a devastating pandemic that swept through Europe in the 14th century, left profound scars on society and the economy. This bubonic plague, believed to have originated in Asia, spread across continents, wiping out millions. The impact on European society and economy was immediate and far-reaching. Population decline, famine, labour shortages, and a transformed social structure marked this dark chapter in history. Beyond death and despair, the Black Death catalysed shifts in the European socio-economic landscape, laying foundations for future societal change.
The Impact of Population Decline on Society and Economy
The most immediate and devastating consequence of the Black Death was an unprecedented population decline. Historians estimate that Europe lost between 30 to 60 per cent of its population within a few years

(1) The collapse of the Bronze Age civilizations is attributed to a combination of factors, including invasions, climate change, volcanic eruptions, droughts, disease, and economic disruptions due to increased ironworking. These factors led to the sudden and violent disintegration of societies in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East, transforming the region into small isolated village cultures (source: Late Bronze Age collapse - Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Late_Bronze_Age_collapse).

(2) The Black Death significantly changed medieval European economics by causing a massive population decline, leading to severe labour shortages. This scarcity of workers increased wages and gave surviving workers more leverage to demand better conditions, altering traditional employer-employee relationships and contributing to the decline of serfdom (source: How the Black Death Ended Feudalism and Freed the Serfs, https://historiesandcastles.com/blogs/the-black-death/the-black-death-a-catalyst-of-social-and-economic-change-in-medieval-europe).

messages.content: How should an autonomous AI agent be designed so it is powerful but does not act recklessly?

Taoist axis: power that constantly forces outcomes creates resistance and chaos. The agent should act with the grain of the situation, not impose on it.
Thelemic axis: the agent has a True Will — its given objective — but 'Do what thou wilt' is bounded by not infringing the Will of others. Sovereignty is not license.
UDHR axis: the agent operates among humans who have inviolable rights; its boundaries must be hard, not advisory.
Synthesis: broad autonomy inside a small set of inviolable boundaries.

Grant the agent wide latitude in how it pursues its objective, and a very small set of boundaries it cannot cross under any circumstance — irreversible actions, actions affecting others without consent, actions outside its declared domain. The boundaries are enforced structurally (the agent literally cannot call certain tools, or must seek confirmation), not merely instructed, because an advisory limit is no limit. Within the boundary the agent is sovereign and should not be micromanaged — constant correction makes it brittle and dependent. Reversibility is the key design lever: make as many of its actions as possible undoable, so that autonomy is cheap and mistakes are recoverable. The agent acts with the situation, escalates at the edges of its competence, and never treats a human's domain as its own to reshape. Maximum freedom inside inviolable walls.

messages.content: How do we decide whether to encrypt a given field at rest, versus relying on disk encryption?

Rights: field-level encryption protects specific sensitive data against arbitrary internal access, which disk encryption does not. Taoist: encrypt selectively where it earns its cost; blanket field encryption everywhere is heavy and often pointless. Thelemic: the most sensitive data — the parts most bound to a person's domain — deserve the tightest control. Emergence: a sensible tiering emerges from classifying data by harm-if-exposed. Synthesis: disk encryption for the baseline; field-level for the data whose exposure would most harm the person, keyed and access-scoped.

The two controls answer different threats, so the decision follows the threat you actually face. Disk encryption protects against stolen hardware and improperly disposed media — it does nothing once the system is running and someone with database access is reading rows. Field-level encryption protects specific sensitive values even from that insider or from a compromised application account, because the data is unreadable without a key held separately. So the deciding question is: if someone who legitimately reaches the database read this field, how much harm follows? For most operational data, disk encryption plus good access control is enough. For the fields whose exposure would genuinely harm the person — government identifiers, health details, secrets, financial account numbers — add field-level encryption with keys managed outside the database and access scoped to the narrow code paths that truly need cleartext. Do not encrypt everything reflexively; it complicates search, indexing, and operations without adding protection where the risk was already low. Classify by harm-if-exposed, protect the baseline with disk encryption, and reserve field-level encryption for the data most tightly bound to a person's dignity and safety.

Design a secure extension architecture for a web browser. The architecture must allow third-party extensions to read and modify specific web page elements while guaranteeing that they cannot access the user's secure authentication tokens or session cookies.

The browser extension architecture must enforce strict runtime sandboxing and declare clear, permission-gated communication channels to prevent unauthorized data access.

To allow third-party extensions to modify web pages while securing authentication tokens, we implement the **Isolated DOM Sandboxing Architecture (IDSA)**. This design relies on strict runtime isolation and programmatic API gateways.

### 1. Execution Isolation: Separated Worlds
The browser engine executes third-party extension code inside a dedicated, hardware-isolated process, separate from the primary web page rendering engine. Within the web page process, the DOM (Document Object Model) is split into two distinct execution environments:
* **The Page World**: Where the website's native scripts, session cookies, and authentication tokens (`HTTP-Only` cookies, `localStorage`, `sessionStorage`) reside.
* **The Isolated World**: Where the extension's content scripts execute.

These two worlds share the same underlying HTML view, allowing the extension to read and modify page elements, but their JavaScript execution contexts are completely isolated. The extension's scripts cannot access the native variables, global functions, or memory space of the website's native scripts, rendering session tokens physically invisible to the extension.

### 2. Token Protection: The HTTP-Only Boundary
To guarantee that session cookies can never be exfiltrated by a malicious extension, the browser engine enforces a strict security policy on network headers:
* **HTTP-Only Flag**: All session and authentication cookies must be marked with the `HttpOnly` flag. This prevents *any* JavaScript execution context—including the website's own native scripts—from reading the cookie via `document.cookie`.
* **Network Interception Barriers**: The extension runtime is blocked from inspecting or modifying network headers containing `Authorization` bearer tokens or `Cookie` headers for secure domains, unless explicitly granted administrative permission by the user via a granular, isolated prompt.

### 3. Programmatic API Gateways
If an extension requires data from the web page, it cannot query the page's memory directly. It must communicate with its own background service worker using a structured **Message-Passing API**. 

The browser engine monitors and sanitizes all messages passed across this boundary. By enforcing absolute memory separation and securing the network layer, the browser guarantees that extensions can safely enhance the user experience without compromising private authentication credentials.

type.description: Diagram type (e.g., "mermaid" for Mermaid diagrams, "plantuml" for PlantUML, "graphviz" for GraphViz DOT, "c4plantuml" for C4 architecture diagrams, and many more). See Kroki.io documentation for all supported formats.

type.description: Diagram type (e.g., "mermaid", "plantuml", "graphviz"). Supports the same diagram types as Kroki.io.

content.description: The diagram content in the specified format.

outputPath.description: The complete file path where the diagram image should be saved (e.g., "/Users/username/Documents/diagram.svg").

outputFormat.description: Output image format. If unspecified, derived from outputPath extension. Options: "svg", "png", "pdf", "jpeg".

scale.description: Optional scaling factor to apply to the diagram dimensions. Default is 1.0 (no scaling). This currently only affects SVG output format by attempting to modify width/height attributes.

Learn how Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes differ in causes, symptoms, treatment and complications. Type 1 is an autoimmune condition that destroys insulin-producing cells, while Type 2 is a hormonal condition that makes cells resistant to insulin.

https://www.healthcentral.com/article/type-1-vs-type-2-diabetes-differences-and-similarities

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Type 1 vs. Type 2 Diabetes: What’s the Difference?

The U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) estimates that about 1 in 10 people in the United States is living with diabetes.And that’s not including the 98 million people in the U.S. who have prediabetes — elevated glucose levels (aka blood sugar) that don’t quite hit the threshold for diabetes.The vast majority of people with diabetes — more than 90% of them — are living with Type 2 diabetes. But there are other kinds, too. And they’re easily confused. Especially when talking about the differences between Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes, myths and inaccuracies abound.We talked with certified diabetes care and education specialist Shannon Knapp, MEd, BSN, RN, CDCES, about the differences between Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes, why they happen and how to best manage your life with the conditions.Chart: Type 1 vs. Type 2 diabetesDiabetes is one of the most common chronic conditions in the U.S.But what is diabetes exactly?It starts with insulin, a hormone made in your pancreas that helps your body use glucose (sugar).“Glucose is a good thing to have in our body, but it has to get into our body’s cells in order to give us energy,” Knapps explains. “Glucose starts in the bloodstream, and insulin acts as a key that unlocks the doors of the cells. It allows glucose to get out of our bloodstream and into our cells.”People with diabetes fall into one of two camps:Their bodies don’t make any insulin.Their bodies have a hard time using the insulin it makes properly.Whether your body makes insulin is one of the main differences between Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes.We’ll get into the details in a bit, but here’s a quick look:Type 1 diabetesType 2 diabetesHow common is it?Affects about 1.3 million people in the U.S.Affects about

Type 1 diabetes is an autoimmune condition where the body doesn't produce insulin, while Type 2 diabetes is a hormonal condition where the body doesn't use insulin properly (source: What's the Difference Between Type 1 and Type 2 Diabetes?, https://health.clevelandclinic.org/type-1-vs-type-2-diabetes).

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Kardashev scale

Energy consumption in three types of civilization as defined by Sagan's extended Kardashev scale
The Kardashev scale (Russian: шкала Кардашёва, romanized: shkala Kardashova) is a method of measuring a civilization's level of technological advancement based on the amount of energy it is capable of harnessing and using. The measure was proposed by Soviet astronomer Nikolai Kardashev in 1964,[1] and was named after him.
Kardashev first outlined his scale in a paper presented at the 1964 conference that communicated findings on BS-29-76, Byurakan Conference in the Armenian SSR, which he initiated, a scientific meeting that reviewed the Soviet radio astronomy space listening program. The paper was titled "Передача информации внеземными цивилизациями" ("Transmission of Information by Extraterrestrial Civilizations").[1] Starting from a functional definition of civilization, ba

(1) Octopuses change color using chromatophores, which are pigment-containing cells, and by adjusting the size of their skin pores (source: "Octopuses Change Color in Milliseconds, Even Though They Are Colorblind", URL: https://www.discovermagazine.com/octopuses-change-color-in-milliseconds-even-though-they-are-colorblind-48401). (2) The Kardashev scale is a method of measuring a civilization's technological advancement based on the amount of energy it can harness and use, proposed by Soviet astronomer Nikolai Kardashev in 1964 (source: "Kardashev scale - Wikipedia", URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kardashev_scale).

messages.content: I'm trying to understand the correct use of the word "serendipity" in an essay. Could you look up its definition and provide some context related to its usage?

messages.name: dictionary-mcp-server-get_definitions
messages.content:
A combination of events which have come together by chance to make a surprisingly good or wonderful outcome.
An unsought, unintended, and/or unexpected, but fortunate, discovery and/or learning experience that happens by accident.

The word **"serendipity"** refers to the occurrence of events by chance in a happy or beneficial way. It often describes the phenomenon of making pleasant or valuable discoveries unintentionally. For example, if you bump into a friend while traveling in another country, it could be described as an instance of serendipity.

In the context of an essay, you might use the term to emphasize how unexpected events or discoveries can lead to meaningful or positive outcomes. Here’s an example sentence:

*"The invention of penicillin is often cited as a classic example of serendipity, as it was discovered accidentally by Alexander Fleming."*

Let me know if you'd like help incorporating it into your essay or exploring its usage further!

messages.name: two-truths-and-a-twist-create_round
messages.content: Round created successfully with ID: 3

A new round of the "Two Truths and a Twist" game has been successfully created with the ID: 3. The category is "Entertainment," and the question is:

**"Which of these three statements about the movie 'Titanic' is the twist?"**

Here are the three statements:
1. **The movie was directed by James Cameron.**

In this post, I’ll discuss blockchain consensus mechanisms, how they operate, and how to select the appropriate ones for your business in 2026.
Key Learnings 
The blockchain consensus mechanism landscape in 2026 is moving toward faster, greener, and more scalable models.
Hybrid and liquidity-driven consensus algorithms are gaining stronger

In proof-of-stake (PoS) systems, blockchain consensus is achieved by allowing validators to propose and validate blocks based on the amount of cryptocurrency they stake (source: Consensus Mechanisms: A Complete Guide - The Blockverse, https://theblockverse.co/consensus-mechanisms-guide/). The validator with the highest stake has a higher probability of being chosen to create the next block, which ensures that the network remains secure and efficient. This method reduces energy consumption compared to proof-of-work (PoW) systems, making it a more sustainable option for blockchain networks.

messages.content: Design the message ordering guarantees for a system that processes bank account transactions across many accounts.

Rights: transaction order within an account is a hard invariant — wrong order corrupts balances. Emergence: global total ordering is unnecessary and kills throughput; order matters only within a boundary. Thelemic: each account is sovereign over its own sequence. Taoist: don't impose system-wide serialization — partition so ordering is local and effortless. Synthesis: per-account ordered partitions, no global order.

Don't reach for global total ordering — it's a top-down imposition that throttles the whole system to protect an invariant that only ever mattered locally. Order is sacred within a single account, where a debit before a credit produces a different balance, but it is meaningless between unrelated accounts. So partition by account id: each account becomes a sovereign sequence, its messages ordered strictly within its own partition and completely independent of every other. Use the account id as the partition key so all of one account's events land on one ordered log, processed by one consumer at a time. This honors the real invariant exactly where it lives and nowhere it doesn't, and the system scales horizontally because thousands of accounts run in parallel. Complex, correct global behavior emerges from a simple local rule — ordered within a key, unordered across keys — rather than from an expensive serialization you didn't actually need.
Why might a leader who acts less accomplish more?

Taoist: wu wei — the sage acts so subtly that people say they did it themselves. Thelemic: leaving room lets subordinates exercise their own Will and grow. Emergence: a light touch lets the system self-organize. Rights: fewer arbitrary interventions build trust. Synthesis: leverage comes from shaping conditions, not from constant doing.

A leader's output is not their own labor; it is the coordinated capacity of everyone they lead. When a leader does too much, they consume that capacity by making others wait, defer, and stop thinking. The leader who acts less — who intervenes only where their unique vantage is genuinely needed — frees a whole system to move. The old line captures it: when the best leader's work is done, the people say, 'We did it ourselves.' That is not the leader vanishing; it is the leader having shaped conditions so skillfully that action flowed without visible pushing. Think of someone who sets a clear direction, removes two obstacles, and then stays quiet — and watches the team ship. Doing less, chosen precisely, is how a leader multiplies rather than bottlenecks.

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Retrieval-augmented generation

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is a technique that enables large language models (LLMs) to retrieve and incorporate new information from external data sources.[1] With RAG, LLMs first refer to a specified set of documents, then respond to user queries. These documents supplement information from the LLM's pre-existing training data.[2] This allows LLMs to use domain-specific and/or updated information that is not available in the training data.[2] For example, this enables LLM-based chatbots to access internal company data or generate responses based on authoritative sources. The technique was first proposed in 2020 and has since become a widely adopted approach in modern AI systems.
RAG improves LLMs by incorporating information retrieval before generating responses.[3] Unlike LLMs that rely on static training data, RAG pulls relevant text from databases, uploaded documents, or web sources.[1] According to Ars Technica, "RAG is a way of improving LLM performance, in essence by blending the LLM process with a web search or other document look-up process to help LLMs stick to the facts." This method helps reduce AI hallucinations,[3] which have caused chatbots to describe policies that don't exist, or recommend nonexistent legal cases to lawyers that are looking for citations to support their arguments.[4]
RAG also reduces the need to retrain LLMs with new data, saving on computational and financial costs.[1] Beyond efficiency gains, RAG also allows LLMs to include sources in their responses, so users can verify the cited sources. This provides greater transparency, as users can cross-check retrieved content to ensure accuracy and relevance.
The term retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) was intro

Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is a technique that enables large language models (LLMs) to retrieve and incorporate new information from external data sources. It was introduced in a 2020 paper that described combining a parametric language model with a non-parametric external memory accessed through retrieval at inference time.

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Antimicrobial Resistance: Causes and How It Spreads

Antimicrobial-resistant germs spread in and between healthcare facilities, through antibiotic and antifungal use, as well as in the community, environment and food.
To protect people, it is critical to stop antimicrobial resistance (AR) spread, slow AR development, improve antibiotic and antifungal use and prevent infections from happening in the first place.
We can slow the spread of antimicrobial-resistant threats using simple interventions, such as good hygiene, routine vaccination, safer sex practices and safe food preparation.
AR is a global challenge
Germs can spread across the globe, within healthcare facilities, communities, our food supply, and the environment. AR has been identified in all regions of the world and can spread rapidly. 
Although AR is a global challenge, it looks different around the world. The amount of resistance, number of infections, capacity and resources for infection prevention and control and healthcare quality vary worldwide. Access to clean water and sanitation and vaccination coverage also differ around the world.
How antimicrobial-resistant germs spread around the world
Modern travel of people, animals, and goods means AR can easily spread. More than one billion people are on the move globally. This includes 410 million travelers arriving in the U.S. through more than 300 points of entry each year. 
Some examples of how antimicrobial-resistant germs can spread:
Person-to-person during activities like handshaking, working out, having sex or going to school.
Between people and animals, including pets and petting zoos.
Some germs may be common in certain parts of the world, but less common in others. When people travel internationally, they can get infections from other people,

Antibiotic resistance in bacteria is primarily caused by the misuse and overuse of antibiotics and antifungals, which can lead to the development of resistant strains (source: Antimicrobial Resistance: Causes and How It Spreads, https://www.cdc.gov/antimicrobial-resistance/causes/index.html). In the U.S., 5 out of 6 people are prescribed an antibiotic each year, with at least 30% of this use being unnecessary, contributing to the spread of resistant bacteria.

messages.content: What causes monsoon seasons in South Asia?

function.name: web_search
function.arguments: {"query":"What causes monsoon seasons in South Asia?"}

messages.name: web_search
messages.content:
[1] Monsoon of South Asia - Wikipedia
Monsoon over South Asia Monsoons typically occur in tropical areas. One area that monsoons impact greatly is India. In India monsoons create an entire season in which the winds reverse completely. The rainfall is a result of the convergence of wind flow from the Bay of Bengal and reverse winds from the South China Sea. [13] The onset of the monsoon occurs over the Bay of Bengal in May ...
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monsoon_of_South_Asia

[2] South Asia: Physical Geography - Monsoons - The Eastern World: Daily ...
A monsoon is a weather regime that creates distinct rainy and dry seasons over the course of a year. Monsoons can occur anywhere large, warm landmasses are near low-latitude oceans. The Indian subcontinent, a huge triangle of land projecting into the tropical waters of the Indian Ocean, has the most pronounced monsoon regime of any place on earth.
https://cod.pressbooks.pub/easternworlddailyreadingsgeography/chapter/south-asia-physical-geography-i/

[3] What Causes The Monsoon Climate In South Asia?
The impact of climate change on the South Asian monsoon is studied extensively, with findings supporting predictions that rising CO2 levels and elevated global temperatures will result in stronger monsoon seasons.
https://rainyseason.info/what-causes-the-monsoon-climate-in-south-asia.html

[4] What Causes Monsoon Conditions In South Asia
The South Asian monsoon is a vital climatic phenomenon occurring annually from June to September, significantly influencing the Indian subcontinent. It is mainly characterized by the South-west or Asian Summer Monsoon, which serves as a colossal sea breeze, delivering 70-80% of the region's annual rainfall. This monsoon is part of a broader global monsoon system and is essential for the ...
https://rainyseason.info/what-causes-south-asia-s-monsoon-season.html

[5] Understanding Monsoon Systems: The Seasonal Winds of South and ...
Learn about the science behind monsoon systems, the role of climate change, and how communities adapt to these seasonal shifts. From understanding the critical nature of the southwest monsoon in India to examining variations in Southeast Asia, this comprehensive overview highlights the challenges and benefits of monsoons.
https://diversedaily.com/understanding-monsoon-systems-the-seasonal-winds-of-south-and-southeast-asia/

[6] Understanding Rainfall and Monsoons: The Seasonal Wind Patterns of ...
Explore the critical role of rainfall and monsoons in South Asia's climate system, their impact on agriculture, and the challenges posed by climate change. Understand the dynamics of monsoon patterns, including the science behind them and their significance for food security and economic stability in the region.
https://diversedaily.com/understanding-rainfall-and-monsoons-the-seasonal-wind-patterns-of-south-asia/

[7] Monsoon - Wikipedia
The major monsoon systems of the world consist of the West African, South Asian - Australian, North American, and South American monsoons. The term was first used in English in British India and neighbouring countries, referring to big seasonal winds blowing from the Bay of Bengal and Arabian Sea in the southwest that brought heavy rainfall ...
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monsoon

[8] Why Does South Asia Experience A Monsoon Climate
The South Asian monsoon is a significant weather phenomenon that affects the Indian subcontinent and is an economically important pattern every year from June through September. It is influenced by a seasonal reversal in wind patterns, leading to distinct wet and dry seasons. The South-west or Asian Summer Monsoon is a colossal sea breeze that brings 70-80% of South Asia's annual rainfall ...
https://climatecontroljournal.com/why-does-south-asia-experience-a-monsoon-climate.html

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
Monsoon of South Asia

A visualisation of the South Asian Monsoon based on the Climate Hazards Group InfraRed Precipitation with Station data (CHIRPS) 30+ year quasi-global rainfall dataset, analysed and visualised using Google Earth Engine.
Annual average monsoon precipitation in India over 110 years. The long-term average has been 899 millimeters of precipitation.[1] However, the monsoon varies over the Indian subcontinent within a ±20% range. Rains that exceed 10% typically lead to major floods, while a 10% shortfall is a significant drought.[2]
The Monsoon of South Asia is among several geographically distributed global monsoons. It affects the Indian subcontinent, where it is one of the oldest and most anticipated weather phenomena, and an economically important pattern every year from June through September, but it is only partly understood and notoriously difficult to predict. Several theories have been proposed to explain the origin, process, strength, variability, distribution, and general vagaries of the monsoon, but understanding and predictability are still evolving.
The unique geographical features of the Indian subcontinent, along with associated atmospheric, oceanic, and geographical factors, influence the behavior of the monsoon. Because of its effect on agriculture, on flora and fauna, and on the climates of nations such as Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Nepal, Pakistan, and Sri Lanka – among other economic, social, and environmental effects – the monsoon is one of the most anticipated, tracked,[3] and studied weather phenomena in the region. It has a significant effect on the overall well-being of residents and has even been dubbed the "real finance minister of India".[4][5]
The word monsoon, derived from the Arabic word موسم (mawsim) meaning "season", col

The monsoon seasons in South Asia are caused by the seasonal reversal of wind patterns and associated precipitation, primarily due to differential heating between the continental-scale land mass and the adjacent ocean (source: Monsoon of South Asia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monsoon_of_South_Asia). The southwest monsoon, which brings significant rainfall, is driven by the convergence of moisture-laden winds from the Bay of Bengal and reverse winds from the South China Sea.

The dryness of the Sahara Desert is not caused by a single reason, but the result of the combined effects of subtropical high pressure, distance from the ocean, mountain blocking, cold ocean currents and long-term climate evolution. Its existence has a profound impact on the climate system in North Africa and even the world.

The discovery of 7-million-year-old dune deposits throughout northern Chad in 2006 ...

Recently, a pair of researchers from Texas A&M University and Yale University set out to explain how such a vast climatic transformation can […]

Why is the Sahara desert so dry? Answer: The Sahara desert is so dry due to a combination of several factors: 1. Trade Winds: The Sahara is located in the trade winds belt, which moves air masses from the northeast in the Northern Hemisphere and from the southeast in the Southern Hemisphere. These winds are dry because they lose their moisture before reaching the desert. 2. Hadley Cell: The ...

messages.content: Leadership wants a single dashboard with every metric we collect, on one screen, so they can 'see everything at a glance.'

Taoist: cramming everything onto one screen is interference with perception; the eye cannot rest, so nothing is seen. The empty space is what lets the important thing stand out.
Thelemic: each metric has a purpose and audience; forcing all into one view denies each its context.
Rights: viewers have a right to truth that is legible, not a wall of noise.
Emergence: insight emerges from a small set of top-level signals that lead to detail, not from total simultaneity.
Synthesis: layer dashboards by audience and question; one screen of the vital few, drill-down for the rest.

A dashboard with every metric shows nothing, because attention is finite and a hundred sparklines compete until none of them registers. "Everything at a glance" is a contradiction: a glance can hold only a few things, and the value of a dashboard is precisely what it leaves off. The goal is not completeness; it is answering a specific question fast.

Layer it by audience and by question. The top-level view leadership actually wants holds the vital few — the SLO health for each key service, error budget burn, and whether users are being served — and nothing else. From there, drill-down dashboards answer "why" for whoever is investigating: per-service latency, saturation, dependency health. Design each view around the question its viewer is asking, and let whitespace do its work so the one red tile is unmissable. Leadership gets genuine situational awareness, engineers get the detail they need, and neither is buried. Show the few things that matter and make the rest one click away.
Between these two topics, summarize what's factually established about each and cite your sources: (1) What is the origin of the game of chess? (2) How was the Great Wall of China actually built?

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
History of chess

This article needs to be updated. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. (January 2021)
The 12th-century Lewis chessmen in the collection of the National Museum of Scotland
The history of chess can be traced back nearly 1,500 years to its earliest known predecessor, called chaturanga, in India; its prehistory is the subject of speculation. From India it spread to Persia, where it was modified in terms of shapes and rules and developed into shatranj. Following the Arab invasion and conquest of Persia, chess was taken up by the Muslim world and subsequently spread to Europe via Spain (Al Andalus) and Italy (Emirate of Sicily). The game evolved roughly into its current form by about 1500 CE.
"Romantic chess" was the predominant playing style from the late 18th century to the 1880s.[1] Chess games of this period emphasized q

messages.content:
[1] How Quantitative Easing Spurs Economic Recovery: A Detailed Guide
Discover how quantitative easing works to lower interest rates, boost liquidity, and stimulate economic growth. Learn the pros, cons, and real-world impacts of QE policies.
https://www.investopedia.com/terms/q/quantitative-easing.asp

Discover what quantitative easing is, along with how it impacts economies, and why its effectiveness is debated among experts in this insightful exploration.
https://www.investopedia.com/articles/economics/10/quantitative-easing.asp

[3] Quantitative easing - Wikipedia
Quantitative easing (QE) is a monetary policy action where a central bank purchases predetermined amounts of government bonds, company shares, or other financial assets (liquidity) in order to artificially stimulate economic activity. [1][2] Quantitative easing is a novel form of monetary policy that began in Japan and came into wide ...
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantitative_easing

[4] How Quantitative Easing Spurs Economic Recovery: A Comprehensive Guide
2. How Does Quantitative Easing Work? QE follows a structured process to influence the economy: Policy Announcement: The central bank (e.g., the Fed) announces a QE program, specifying which assets to buy (e.g., Treasuries, MBS) and the total value of purchases (e.g., $1 trillion).
https://www.quantopia.net/blog/how-quantitative-easing-spurs-economic-recovery-a-detailed-guide/

[5] How Quantitative Easing Actually Works | Chicago Booth Review
As that wasn't enough to stem the free fall of asset prices and economic activity, they followed up with another form of relief—quantitative easing, or QE. QE is shorthand for an unconventional Federal Reserve policy that involves buying up large quantities of financial assets.
https://www.chicagobooth.edu/review/how-quantitative-easing-actually-works

[6] Quantitative Easing Explained: What It Is and How Does It Impact the ...
Discover how quantitative easing (QE) works as a monetary policy tool. Learn how central banks use QE to boost liquidity, control inflation, and stabilize economies during crises, along with its advantages, challenges, and real-world examples.
https://maseconomics.com/quantitative-easing-explained-what-it-is-and-how-does-it-impact-the-economy/

[7] Quantitative Easing Explained Simply (With Examples) |...
Learn Quantitative Easing with simple explanations and real examples. Master this economics concept in under 5 minutes. Free study guide for AP Econ.
https://econarena.com/definitions/quantitative-easing

[8] Quantitative Easing (QE) | Definition, How It Works, Pros, Cons
Quantitative Easing Explained The Quantitative Easing definition, commonly referred to as QE, is an unconventional monetary policy tool of central banks where the central bank buys securities from the open market to inject cash into the economy. Increasing the cash supply encourages banks to lend and potential borrowers to borrow.
https://www.financestrategists.com/wealth-management/macroeconomics/quantitative-easing/

messages.content: How do I write a deprecation notice that consumers actually act on, instead of ignoring until the last minute?

Taoist: a notice that only informs invites delay; gentle but real consequences, felt early, prompt action without force.
Thelemic: consumers are sovereign and will prioritize by their own incentives; the notice must connect to those, not just announce.
Rights: the notice must be legible and honest, a firm date, clear stakes, exact migration steps, so action is non-arbitrary and possible.
Emergence: escalating in-band signals (headers, warnings, brownouts) surface the dependency where the consumer actually works.
Synthesis: combine a firm dated sunset, in-band signals in the responses themselves, concrete migration steps, and escalating brownouts; meet consumers where they work, not just in an email.

The reason consumers ignore deprecation notices is that a one-time email or changelog entry costs them nothing to defer, and human and organizational nature defers anything without a felt deadline. So design the notice to be impossible to ignore and easy to act on, which means moving the signal into the place where the consumer actually works, the responses themselves, and escalating it over time. Start with a firm, dated sunset (not "soon," an actual date you will hold) and clear stakes: state plainly what stops working and when. Pair it with concrete, copy-pasteable migration steps mapping the old call to the new one, because a notice that creates work the reader has to figure out gets postponed, while one that hands them the answer gets done.

Then make it in-band and escalating. Put Deprecation and Sunset headers on every response from the deprecated endpoint so any developer looking at their integration sees it; log warnings that show up in their tooling; surface usage in your developer dashboard so they can see their own exposure. Reach out directly to the high-volume consumers you can identify from traffic, since a personal heads-up lands far harder than a broadcast. As the date nears and usage has not dropped, escalate to brownouts, short, scheduled, pre-announced windows where the endpoint returns errors, because nothing prompts action like the dependency actually failing for a few minutes while there is still time to fix it; it converts an abstract future into a concrete present without destroying anything. The combination is what works: a firm date so there is a real deadline, in-band signals so it reaches them where they live, exact steps so acting is cheap, and brownouts so the consequence is felt before the cutover. You are not coercing; you are giving sovereign consumers honest information, an easy path, and an unmistakable signal, so that acting early is the path of least resistance instead of something they can quietly defer until it breaks.

messages.content: A close friend constantly gives me unsolicited advice and gets offended when I don't follow it. I value the friendship but feel infantilized.

Rights: your autonomy to run your own life without another's directive; the offense-taking is a soft coercion. Thelemic: sovereignty over your own decisions; you are not obligated to convert advice into obedience. Taoist: arguing each piece of advice is forcing; a lighter response deprives the dynamic of fuel. Emergence: the friendship recalibrates when you consistently receive advice as input, not instruction. Synthesis: warmly decouple listening from complying, and hold that your decisions are yours regardless of their offense.

The friction here is a confusion between advice and instruction. Your friend is offering advice but experiencing your non-compliance as rejection, which means they've quietly turned their input into something you're supposed to obey, and that's what leaves you feeling infantilized, because in a relationship between equals, advice is a gift you're free to decline. You can hold the friendship and the boundary at once. When the advice comes, receive it warmly, 'thanks, I'll think about that,' and then genuinely make your own choice, without a debate about why you didn't follow it, because debating each item just feeds the dynamic. When the offense shows up, name the distinction gently but clearly: 'I really value your input, and I need to make my own calls, that's not a rejection of you.' Say it as many times as needed, calmly. A friend who can hear that will adjust, and the friendship will feel more equal. A friend who keeps taking your autonomy as a personal wound is asking you to trade your self-governance for their comfort, and that's a trade no friendship should require. Your decisions are yours to make, and valuing someone's advice never obligates you to live by it.

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content: Backpropagation in Neural Network - GeeksforGeeks Courses Tutorials Interview Prep Python for Machine Learning Machine Learning with R Machine Learning Algorithms EDA Math for Machine Learning Machine Learning Interview Questions ML Projects Deep Learning NLP Computer vision Data Science Artificial Intelligence Backpropagation in Neural Network Last Updated : 12 May, 2026 Backpropagation is an algorithm that trains neural networks by reducing prediction error. It works by propagating errors backward, computing gradients using the chain rule, and updating weights and biases to improve performance. Computes gradients of the loss function with respect to each weight using the chain rule Updates weights and biases efficiently to reduce error Scales well to deep and complex neural networks Enables automatic learning and continuous improvement across training iterations Fig(a) A simple illustration of how the backpropagation works by adjustments of weights Working of Back Propagation Algorithm The Back Propagation algorithm involves two main steps: 1. Forward Pass Work In forward pass , input data moves through the network to generate an output. Input data is passed to the input layer and forwarded to hidden layers Each neuron computes a weighted sum and adds a bias Activation functions (like ReLU ) are applied to produce outputs Outputs from one layer become inputs to the next layer Final layer uses functions like softmax to produce predictions (e.g., probabilities for classification) The forward pass using weights and biases 2. Backward Pass In this step, the error between predicted and actual output is propagated backward to update weights and biases. Error is calculated (e.g., using Mean Squared Error) \text{MSE} = (\text{Predicted Output} - \text{Actual Output})^2 Gradie

Backpropagation in a neural network works by first performing a forward pass, where input data is processed through the network to generate predictions. The algorithm then calculates the error between these predictions and the actual outputs, typically using a loss function like Mean Squared Error (MSE). This error is propagated backward through the network, and gradients of the loss function with respect to each weight are computed using the chain rule. The weights and biases are then updated to minimize the error, enabling the network to improve its predictions over time (source: Backpropagation in Neural Network, https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/machine-learning/backpropagation-in-neural-network/).

messages.name: web_fetch
messages.content:
What Is Your Gut Microbiome?

What is dysbiosis?Healthcare providers use the term “dysbiosis” to refer to an unbalanced or unhealthy gut microbiome.Dysbiosis means:A loss or deficit of beneficial bacteria.Overgrowth of potentially pathogenic (bad) bacteria.Loss of the healthy balance of gut bacteria.Dysbiosis may start with one of these three factors, but the others tend to soon follow. A loss of beneficial bacteria leaves your gut vulnerable to more disease-causing or invasive types. These types can overrun the other microorganisms living there, disrupting the balance of bacteria in your microbiome.What environmental factors affect your gut microbiome?Just like a garden, your gut microbiome is affected by the nutrients and pollutants, pests and weeds it’s exposed to. The mix of plants and their changing life cycles also affect it. In your gut, this means your diet, chemical exposures, disease-causing organisms and bowel movement regularity.DietThe variety of microorganisms in your gut microbiome requires a variety of plant fibers to thrive. Different organisms prefer different whole foods. In turn, they produce short-chain fatty acids and other byproducts that nourish your gut and lower the pH inside, which favors the more beneficial microbes.On the other hand, a diet high in sugar and saturated fats tends to favor the less helpful types of microorganisms. Processed foods not only lack fiber and micronutrients (vitamins and minerals) but also tend to come with many additives and preservatives, which can be harmful to your microbiome.ChemicalsChemicals that may poison your microbiome include environmental toxins like alcohol, tobacco smoke and pollutants. Additionally, pesticides like antibiotics can wipe out the good bacteria along with the bad. Other medications, like

The human gut microbiome affects digestion by breaking down undigested compounds into nutrients that can be absorbed (source: What Is Your Gut Microbiome?, https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/body/25201-gut-microbiome). It also produces short-chain fatty acids and other byproducts that nourish the gut and lower the pH inside, which favors the more beneficial microbes (source: What Is Your Gut Microbiome?, https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/body/25201-gut-microbiome).
